- Top 5 Must Know Tips - Annapolis Boat Show 2024 - Ep 295 - Lady K Sailing: Kind: captions Language: en from parking to which boats to see to how to get screaming deals at the vendors I want you to have the best experience you can at the boat show I just got back from the Newport boat show my first time there and this year was my first time going to the Miami show and then the big one the best one of them all is coming up in a couple weeks the Annapolis fall show this week five tips for you to have a successful trip to the boat show [Music] this is specifically for anopolis because I've been there so many times now twice with my own boat but it applies basically everywhere tip number one order tickets online you can do this right now this is a lot like checking in at the airport before you arrive to get your boarding pass so you don't have to stand in line if you order the Annapolis boat show tickets from their website they'll give you your ticket immediately like right now they give you a PDF that you can save on your phone do that and then open the PDF on your phone and take a screenshot of it now you have your ticket in your camera roll and everyone knows how to open their camera roll right ordering the tickets online now means no lines when you get there every year I go to the Anapolis show and there are two lines at the Dock Street gate one line of confused people in a big mess trying to figure out what's going on and how to get in and one stream of people walking into the show the mess of people are the ones who need to buy tickets on the spot don't wait until the gate to buy them just go buy them on their website tip number two go early every year I don't have my boat anchored there I travel by car or by air and I only go to the show on the Friday it runs all weekend but Saturday is a total mess and so crowded I really don't have time to appreciate very many of the boats the Sunday the morning's okay but by afternoon half the vendors are sold out and packing up and leaving Friday is key all the vendors will be fully stocked and none of the stuff in the tents or on the boats will be exhausted or stressed out yet tip number three parking at the show it's always a nightmare but if you go on the Friday and show up early you will find a spot there are pay lots that pop up around the show like the one at the school in Eastport right here but I don't like to pay because I'm a cheap sailor I go down the little side roads in Eastport this neighborhood is a wealth of street parking a little restaurants boutiques I love Eastport seafood places Galore if you show up about 30 minutes before the show opens and go to Eastport and circle the blocks you'll find a spot to park for free I promise then it's a 10 or so minute walk over the bridge and if you're lucky you'll get there as the bridge opens to let traffic through that's super cool to watch show up around 9:00 a.m. on Friday morning at Park in Eastport Wander over the bridge to the Show entrance it opens at 10: but you'll be at the front of the line tip number four have a plan the boats at the show get crowded so quick you got to make a plan and really execute it the boat list for all the boats that will be at the show this year is right here check it over and figure out what's important to you the bigger and fancier boats turn into Hangouts for well-dressed marketing people so I usually avoid those boats alt together whichever the biggest and most glamorous cat is it'll be full of people the whole time look over this list and make a list of the top five boats you must see look at their location on the list because it gives you the Dock location and then pull up the show map that you can get right here find the boats on the map and as soon as you walk into the show on Friday morning beine it right to the biggest boat on your list the longest Loa the bigger boats get people on them the fastest get to that boat at 10:05 a.m. and spend 20 or 30 minutes on it then on to the next boat on your list and prioritize them this should assure that you get all five boats on your list before everyone else is on them and getting all crowded and it should basically get you to lunchtime lunch is readily available in a couple places the first and most popular choice is Pussers which is a restaurant that's sort of part of the show you can access it from inside the show up at the front but it'll be busy sometimes it's like a 30 minute wait for a table if you run your day early though you can beat the crowds have breakfast super early like 6:00 and 7:00 a.m. that way you'll be ready for lunch before noon if pusers isn't your flavor or it's too busy just leave the show and head for the roundabout this is completely surrounded by restaurants and a lot of outdoor dining if the weather is nice all of these places are pretty good and you'll have lots to choose from after lunch I usually hit the tents everyone else is still eating or just having their first painkiller oh a painkiller is Pusser's famous rum drink and they flow faster than the dollar bills at this boo you can have a level one two three or four painkiller and from memory they are delicious I don't drink anymore so I don't partake but if you do painkillers are like a write a passage at this show so I hit the tents and I look for boat show only deals a lot of these vendors do that you can find some Screaming cash deals here and there are a lot of startup small name companies you've never heard of just trying to get their first few customers so look around this is also a great place to chat with folks from like victron or battleborn about your electrical system questions every company sends representatives and they're usually pretty knowledable and play with all the chart plotters to see which one has the slowest processor I love doing this while the salesman watches I try to plot an 80 mile day up the coast of Florida and I watch the plotter lag right out after the tents I start to wander aimlessly in tour any boats I haven't seen yet tip number five water taxis and this is the most important one for many of us the best part of the show isn't the boat list or the vendors the best boats at the show aren't actually inside the show at all all they're in the water around the show you see this is the first stop on the post hurricane season cruising route most folks who have outfitted their boats with cool solar arrays and starlink dishes and dingy dbits are headed to the Caribbean and they're not allowed to leave us until November according to their insurance company so they're stuck on the East Coast waiting and what better and more protected place to wait than Anapolis so the harbor around the show gets jam-packed with all sorts of Li boards and cruising boats from every corner of the planet the best thing to do is find a boat to drive around in rent one borrow one whatever you can do tour up Spa Creek all the way to the top and then pop over to Back Creek the boat life in and around Annapolis right now is insane and it doesn't stop when the show closes for the day there are hundreds and hundreds of Cruisers just living their lives right outside the show if you can't get a boat go to the water taxi for the duration of the show there are several Water Taxis running around to take people from their anchored and mored boats to and from the show and the water taxis are happy to take sightseers just pay the small water taxi fee usually in cash so bring some small bills and jump on get comfortable and enjoy the ride as the taxi runs around varying people to and from the show most taxis also have a cooler on board and serve cold beer to the passengers for a nominal cash fee I'm serious you're missing the best part of the PainKillers are famously originally from the famous cruiser hangout the "Soggy Bottom Bar" in the BVI , Pussers is very ordinary at best ( aka a tourist trap ) unfortunately  & soon out of biz , we always find FREE parking impossible so the best option ( so NOT to get expensively  towed !  Annapolis has strict confusing parking laws )  The BEST parking options for the Annapolis Boat Show is at the Naval Academy: 
Navy-Marine Corps Memorial Stadium
The easiest option is to park at the stadium and take a free shuttle to the show. Shuttles run from one hour before the show opens until one hour after it closes. Parking can be pre-purchased for $23 per day, or $46 for oversize vehicles. After pre-sale closes, parking can be purchased at the stadium gate for $25 or $50, but only with a credit card.

now oh hal gate is a narrow tidal strait located in the east river in between queens and wards island at the center of a confluence of the new york upper bay long island sound and the hudson river via the harlem river it was originally named heligot or hell gate by the dutch explorer adrian block in 1614. since that time hundreds of ships have sunk in the narrow channel some historians believe that one in 50 ships trying to run the gauntlet of hell's gate was either damaged or sunk with up to 1 000 ships running aground annually in hell's gate prior to the 1850s during the late 19th century the us army corps of engineers began to blow up the rocks and reefs which plagued the treacherous tidal straight to make it a safer passage however currents can run up to six knots at times and tugs with barges under tow can suddenly appear around the sharp bends of the channel adding to that is the possibility of steep five-foot waves near the 59th street bridge due to the upwelling caused by the subway tunnels running under the river needless to say we planned our transit in accordance with the tides the empire state building look good girl new york city the city of dreams when i was in my late teens early 20s i was dying to live in new york city i wanted to work in fashion and in my mind there was no better place it represented freedom and of course the american dream the place where anyone could become someone in fact when fabio and i started dating i told him i was moving to new york so it wasn't anything serious the 20 year old version of myself was disappointed that i never experienced living in new york i didn't realize i was wearing blinders i didn't know what i didn't know and i wasn't thinking big enough i now recognize dreams are infinite without boundaries and life has become even better than i ever imagined in my wildest fantasies going under the brooklyn bridge unbelievable are you enjoying new york i am it's so beautiful i never thought i never actually even like i don't know dreamed of doing this it wasn't yeah yeah no i mean after seeing some other cruisers with videos of doing it through new york i was you know of course i wanted to do it but great - Nantucket - A Film by Ric Burns: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] [Applause] it has always been one of the special places of the world a tiny windswept spit of land flung far out onto the surging currents of the North Atlantic 30 miles off the coast of Cape Cod a discarded scrap of sand and gravel an afterthought of the last ice age bequeathed by the retreating sheets of ice known to its original inhabitants who first plied it's shallow shoals thousands of years ago as Knut Tucket the faraway place perched precariously on the far edge of the New World close to the Gulf Stream and the continental shelf and the track of migrating whales it has always been a rich and dreaming place scoured by wind and wave hem drowned by treacherous shoals and washed by complex currents at once remote and beckoning insular and cosmopolitan worldly and homebound cut off from the mainland but connected to the rest of the world a place of comings and goings arrivals and departures of passages and transformations of vast ambitions bygone glories and vanished dreams haunted by history in time by memory and imagination and by the sea continuously inhabited by human beings since the sea first froze up around it 5000 years ago the island owes its distinctive poetry and character to the elemental natural and human forces that have shaped it to the epic forces of time and tide to the remarkable people who have come here and to the primal calling they made their own and that would come to define them few enterprises have so captured the imagination or so dominated the identity of an entire people long after it had passed from the scene the epic heyday of American whaling would remain a powerful presence on the tiny island of Nantucket whose extraordinary history remains as palpable to this day and as thrilling linear as the cry of seabirds and the smell of salt air and the thunder of the sea in the course of its long and eventful history the 14 mile long spit of land that Herman Melville called an elbow of sand all beach without a background and st. John to craft for a barren sand bank fertilized by whale oil only has been at one time or another the haunt of Wampanoag fishermen a refuge for persecuted Baptists and Quakers an unsuccessful sheep farm the greatest whaling capital in the world a center of enlightened thought an island ghost town and sea washed Brigadoon that time forgot a sleepy artists colony a thriving summer resort and a bellwether for the conservation and environmental movements an island of new beginnings what the poet and essayist Ralph Waldo Emerson called the nation of Nantucket said proudly apart from the restlessly striving Republic it is epitomized and so often pioneered Nantucket has been a microcosm of America for more than 350 years a magnet and a unique laboratory for some of the most powerful impulses animating the new world spiritual commercial and democratic a place of stark and subtle beauty of endurance and creativity and of continuous transformation and reinvention one of the very best ways to see the island is from the air where you can see the shoals and you can watch them move and you realize the entire environment this is dynamic there's nothing about it that's standing still everything that's being deposited one place is coming from somewhere else nann Tucker was formed by a glacier 20,000 years ago glaciers are conveyor belts for rocks so you end up with this huge pile of rocks and sand at the end of glacier called a terminal moraine and that's what we are on Nantucket we're just standing on 1200 feet of sand so the island itself is very shifty you know it's not gonna be here forever about nineteen thousand years ago the glacier started to retreat the ocean levels start rising six thousand years ago the ocean cuts Nantucket off from the mainland and that's when the magic begins the waves begin to sculpt the island into a gradually emerging Halfmoon shape and Nantucket is giving Beaton's constantly along the south shore by the waves you get those barrier beaches the beaches like none other where it's the ocean coming all the way from Portugal pounding against the sand and so Nantucket has always lived on borrowed time it was created by the sea in as little as four hundred years will be taken by the sea as a consequence it's truly a different kind of Island one of my Nantucket friends used to say if you can see the ocean the ocean could see you one thing the island makes clear is that nature bats last and it's far more powerful than we are if you look at the you are map the island is littered with these wonderfully evocative Native American names and those names in some ways are like those early Nantucket settlers names coffin Starbuck Gardiner Maya comets Consett pampas matica matica sham quays an endless array and we're surrounded by them they're in our systems I think it's important to imagine Wampanoag Nantucket as a thriving active place the populations were extremely healthy they had great food supplies they were living off the ocean they were living off of their surroundings essentially they were thriving and healthy people and they were also extremely welcoming to the Nantucket settlers hoping to rest a livelihood from the island sandy soil the first group of newcomers included a merchant and clothier named Thomas Macey and a lumber trader named Edward Starbuck they were joined by the sprawling families of Tristram coffin and Peter Folger and later by the gardener's the hussies the swains the joys and the chases in the centuries to come their names would be inextricably fused with the history of Nantucket and with the poetry of American whaling and commerce they are here to be farmers raise sheep but then quickly realized their limits to that and then they realized they're surrounded by whales in the winter they had to hire someone to ichabod paddock to teach them how to do it but they also had a native population the whompin augs who had been harvesting whales that had washed up on the shore for centuries and those were the ingredients that led to the blossoming of whaling on Nantucket this was the beginning of the Industrial Age and before petroleum the oil that was lubricating the machines lighting the urban centers of america and europe was whele and before there was Mobil Oil headquarters there was Nantucket and it was Nantucket sperm oil that was making the industrial revolution happen and providing the first global economic engine America would know the early settlers to Nantucket were not Quakers they were nonconformist Baptists other sects and sympathies and they had a Quaker conversion around 1702 with an itinerant minister who came here and a leader of the community Mary Starbuck God at she just got the sense of the inner light this empowerment of the inner light and the fact that the Spirit is living within everyone and then that very quickly came to match up with the whaling endeavor there's something about the Quaker religion that breeds a strength of community that the people in Nantucket were able to build upon they had a very good business sense they were very hard workers they had a cohesive social fabric at home so when the men went off the women kept the community running and stable there's a spirituality to it there is a sense of social cement in control and there's a sense of identity it's a spiritual endeavor it lent itself to a real religious sense of destiny you know Quakers with a vengeance as Melville would call them and I think this was a Quaker war when it came to the sperm whale and it was a little horrifying to see the enthusiasm and sort of his ruthlessness that nantucketers had when it came to whaling genuine as the Quakers egalitarian impulse was nothing about the inner light prevented ship owners from exploiting the local Native American population many of whom were forced to work off debts by providing labor in the Nantucket whale fishery nantucketers would not be able to count for long on their cheap supply of skilled local labor disease had already reduced the island's native population to just 358 when in 1763 an epidemic of yellow fever raged across the island before it was over only 136 whompin ogz remained and by 1800 the Native American presence on the island had been essentially eradicated forcing Quaker merchants to look elsewhere for cheap labor to African Americans free and slave to Native Americans from the mainland and to Portuguese Mariners already beginning to stream in from the other side of the Atlantic - man the Nantucket whale ships now pushing out across every ocean on the planet gradually they push it a little farther until they're now they're going out into the the Gulfstream and they go up almost to the Arctic Circle to the west coast of Africa down around Cape Horn into the great Pacific as they moved ever further out across the globe and tuck its whalemen found themselves increasingly at the forefront of a new era of global enterprise and circumnavigation when Benjamin Franklin on the eve of the revolution published the first map to chart and named the Gulfstream he based his breathtakingly precise projections on information he had obtained from his cousin Timothy Folger a veteran man Tucket whaling captain 18:20 Nantucket was poised to enter what would become its golden age where the entire Pacific would become its backyard this is a country poised to become what America would eventually become a world power and this was now a state-of-the-art industry I mean the ships we think of them as square-rigged quaint little crafts but these things were state-of-the-art decades in development they were not only the perfect factory ship for rendering the oil they could go anywhere they were magnificent exploration vessels and they lasted pretty darn long under horrible conditions and yet it was also a home for several dozen men for 3-4 years by the 1830s and 40s Nantucket was the whaling center of the world and it only lasted for a little more than a century but it's really what has determined so much of what Nantucket is today during Nantucket's heyday is a whaling capital the little world the Quakers created for themselves in the sea but become a crucible of social tolerance free thought and egalitarian belief and a beacon of serious intellectual inquiry and political debate in the summer of 1841 a 23 year old fugitive slave named Frederick Douglass delivered the first fiery speech of his meteoric career as an anti-slavery orator at the annual meeting of the Massachusetts anti-slavery society held on Nantucket at the Athenaeum his words struck home on the island with particular force and nowhere more so than in the district of New Guinea on the outskirts of town home to a thriving community of Cape Verdeans Missourians and free African Americans including Absalom Boston the first African American whalemen to Captain a whaleship in 1847 Ralph Waldo Emerson delivered a ringing series of lectures in the Great Hall to as would distinguished men and women of letters from across the country including Henry David Thoreau Horace Mann and Lucretia coffin Mott a nantucketer the really great thing about Quakerism on Nantucket is what it did to empower women so you have someone like Lucretia coffin Mott for example for whom the Quaker meeting becomes a kind of training ground for speaking up about the moral issues of the day from slavery to women's suffrage so it just empowered women in some remarkable ways to take leadership positions to be educated to assume roles in the community in a natural way that you didn't see on the mainland on a velvety black night in 1847 from the observatory her father had built for her on the roof of the Pacific National Bank on Main Street mariah mitchell made the discovery that would catapult her to world fame as one of the greatest astronomers of the 19th century we always showed to people the photograph of the moon where there is a little crater named after Mariah Mitchell and I always tell people this crater is the size of Nantucket shadows were already starting to fall on Nantucket's glory days as a whaling capital when in the summer of 1852 a 33 year old one-time whalemen and writer named Herman Melville set foot on the island for the first time a year after completing his soaring masterpiece Moby Dick one of the greatest works of literature ever written by an American inspired in large part by the island itself and the extraordinary enterprise that had flourished there so spectacularly with the landless gull that at sunset folds her wings and is rocked to sleep between billows so at nightfall the nantucketer out of sight of land furls his sails and lays him to his rest while under his very pillow rush herds of walruses and whales Herman Melville well a Nantucket's really two things there's a time when it's wailing capital of the world this was big business this was America's first global economy and this little island was the center of it they were sending out dozens upon dozens of ships around the world bringing back incredible wealth to the island sending out their whale oil to the industrial centers around the world and nantucketers were really showing America how it would one day become a world power and then it all blew up whaling began to decline for years the shallow sandbar at the mouth of Nantucket Harbor had proved an increasing impediment to whale ships as the voyages grew longer and the ships grew larger with ever deeper drafts when in 1846 the first of a series of devastating blows rained down upon the industry that had been the islands staff of life for a hundred and fifty years in 1846 the fire breaks out the great fire and destroys a third of Nantucket's town all of the waterfront they rebuilt very quickly but they could see the writing on the wall and so when gold was discovered in California a nantucketers evacuated the island virtually they took their whale ships crammed them full of 49ers sailed around the horn and literally abandoned the ships at the Golden Gate they're still finding whale ships when they build new skyscrapers in downtown San Francisco the real bottom of the whale oil market fell out in the late 1850s when they discovered petroleum in the hills of Pennsylvania that could be turned into a flood of cheap kerosene and almost overnight the whale oil industry went into steep decline 18:59 they discovered petroleum 1861 is the beginning of the Civil War and with the Civil War comes the loss of so many whale ships they were an easy target for the Confederate Raiders plus approximately 45 whale ships were sunk in the two-stone fleets off of Charleston and Savannah the war took a heartbreaking human toll too before it was over some 300 nantucketers had joined the Union Army and gone south 73 of them would never see home again and so the age of whaling on Nantucket faded away and passed into the realm of memory and myth and yet even as the island fell into a deep and dreaming sleep something extraordinary began to occur it's a fascinating period on Nantucket you have this incredible global industry that had just died and then you have basically a ghost town but out of that ghost town Springs this growing awareness about the history that had just passed and that was now slowly starting to fade so you have this great generation of nantucketers who are the children and grandchildren of the Wailers and they're surrounded by history they're surrounded by candle factories by paintings by scrimshaw and these things are in many cases literally in their attics and so they're thinking about the recent past and what do they do they form a Historical Association to preserve that they start to gather all these whaling logs and manuscripts they look into their recent history and genealogy and they start to really build a community out of the ashes of the old community by the dawn of the 20th century a remarkable transformation was underway one that would quicken as the century wore on as Nantucket errs began to reinvent and reimagine their Island redirecting energies that had once gone into whaling into preserving and celebrating its rich history and natural beauty repurposing along the way the islands incomparable historic infrastructure fisherman's shacks and scallop shanties that lined the weather dwarves on the waterfront were converted into artist studios or remodeled and rented to the steadily increasing number of summer visitors around town older houses and commercial properties that bore witness to the islands past were painstakingly rehabilitated and brought to life including an old candle factory at the foot of Broad Street where sperma Ceti oil from the head of sperm winds had once been processed into the brilliant smokeless candles prized by Thomas Jefferson and which now became the source of a different kind of light as the headquarters of Nantucket's Historical Association and the home of the whaling Museum the Gateway and anteroom to the wonders of the best-preserved seaport town in America the entire four century arc of the island stirring history is summarized and preserved and for eloquent structures maintained by the Nantucket Historical Association here at the top of Sunset Hill stands the oldest house the most prominent of the surviving structures from the island's 17th century settlement built in 1686 as a wedding gift to Jethro coffin and his bride Mary Gardiner on Prospect Street just one mile away stands the old mill the oldest working mill in America built in 1746 one of five that once stood like giant sentinels on the hills above the harbor a thrilling architectural monument to the age of agriculture and sail on Nantucket on the proudest stretch of Main Street stands the hadwin house a monumental Greek Revival mansion built by a whaling merchant in 1846 at the varies 'inna of Nantucket Flap open the Quaker men were very religious and when they saw the flapped open blubber it reminded them of the Bible so they call those B leavs the next thing we're going to do is bring up the head cavity of the whale that's where that pacetti oil is now hopefully the head cavity is big enough we can get our buckets in and get it nice and dry but sometimes it's a small head cavity so what we're going to do is pick the smallest member of the crew perhaps you young man over there and we're going to tell you to take off your clothes and we're going to give you a bucket and we're going to sink you into the head cavity of the whale and you get to stay in there until it's nice and dry doesn't that sound like fun yeah you can't wait right the next thing we're going to do is look in the intestine of the whale for this commodity which is literally worth as much as gold if you look at our sperm whale he's one of the few whales that has teeth but only on the bottom of his jaw and he doesn't use them for chewing he uses them for grabbing and his lunch of preference is the giant squid and he Dives down thousands of feet looking for that giant squid and attempts to grab it and swallow it it is so far down in the ocean that nobody has ever witnessed it but sperm whales have been found with suction marks so we know a battle of some type took place now as our whale is digesting that giant squid sometimes the beak can pen penetrate his intestine our whale actually has the capability to put a covering around that beak to protect himself and we're looking for this commodity in his intestine it's known as Amber or Amber gree anybody what know what it's used for perfume it is the fix it in for very fine perfume and considered very lucky if we find it the next thing we're going to do is put wood underneath the tripods to render the rest of the blubber as we've been rendering pieces of skin pieces of teeth are going to come up to the surface we're going to take a giant skimming device kind of a great big version of something you might have in your kitchen and we are going to skim off that residue and and now we no longer need to put wood underneath the tripods we're going to feed it that residue so essentially the poor whale is now cooking itself these are the ghostly images that you would see as we go around the clock rendering all of that blubber and putting it into casks and barrels the last thing we're going to do is bring up the Jawbone of the whale and cut it into pieces and we're going to give each member of the crew a piece of Jawbone a tooth or both and in their free time because it could be weeks between sightings of whales they will take the teeth and using either shark skin or a file smooth it and carve into it a design a picture of a loved one this was never intended to be an art form this was literally busy work between Queen whales anybody know what we call it scrimshaw not sure where the name comes from but if you haven't been upstairs to the second floor just a small part of the collection of scrimshaw that's been given to the Nantucket historical Association over the last 130 years is on display now the captain has been busy he has taken his whale stamp and in his log has recorded every whale that they have got coton and how many barrels and casks of oil have come from each when he's confident that that ship can hold no more oil he's going to give the command to raise the red Homeward Bound flag one of the first things they're going to do is blow up the bricks of the triworks after two or three or four years the bricks are brittle if we're going home around Cape Horn which is what Captain Winslow decided to do we want no extra weight so 4 years later the Edward carry returns to Nantucket and in the middle of the 1800s that was about standard for a oiling Journey the oil will be downloaded onto the docks and sold immediately off the top the owners and backers will take their cut of the profits we then have to figure out how much each member of the crew got now Whalers did not receive a salary in the sense that we know salaries they worked on a system called Lei La Y and it was based on your your knowledge and experience the more knowledge and experience you had the bigger percentage of the profits you could bargain for at the beginning but you only got paid at the end of The Journey so young man for your efforts you were able to get one400 or $69 for 4 years I hope you feel pretty rich okay up the scale to the captain who got over $1,800 for the same four years as for our friend young Joseph he had a really good career as a whaler out of New Bedford as well as Nan Tucket unfortunately in his mid-30s he tripped on a rope aboard a whale ship fell overboard and drowned as for the Edward carry it made several more trips up until 1864 when the Confederate Navy in the Pacific Ocean gathered together three or four whale ships and burn them all down because what they wanted to do was impact the oil reserves of the North during the war between the states so by the middle of the 1800s whaling started to die on N tuck it and it was helped along with several factors first of all our Harbor periodically fills with sand it did in the 1800s the holes of the ships were so deep they could not get over the sand bars so many of the captains said hey we'll go someplace else like New Bedford or Cape Cod and by this time New Bedford was on the rail line so it was much easier to move the oil into the country VIA Rail rather then by ship then the Great Fire of 1846 burned a third of the town in Nantucket down including the whole Warf area then the Gold Rush many of the nantucketers jump ship in California by this time the trip for at least four years they said hey guys you know you go looking for those whales I'll take my chances looking for goals and then 18659 something happened in bille Pennsylvania Oil in the ground and then the last blow is the Civil War some 400 Brave nantucketers went to war about 70 of them perished after the war those that were left on the mainland if they wanted to wail might have gone to New Bedford or Cape Cod New Bedford wailed right up until the 1920s or maybe they just got another career so by the middle of the 1800s wailing was pretty much dead on Nantucket we sat out here on our little island thinking boy there's got to be something you know what do you do on a little island like this and when we started to look around because we were so poor and didn't tear anything down we had the largest intact Seaport in America and that's when we started thinking on a lovely day like this would all of you come out and visit us and you do and we really appreciate it because you're all part of our new economy that we base on tourism so that's our trip for today and I'm happy to answer any questions you might have for

- The Ottisons:  A True Nantucket Story: Kind: captions Language: en foreign [Music] stepping on to this property is like stepping back in time the otisons myself my brother and my father built all these structures has been all constructed basically of Salvage wreckage from the shores and Nantucket we did the best we could with what we had we were approached way back what were we going to do with the property developers started knocking on our door they could make us very wealthy and we weren't about to leave the house we created the land we created that was never going to be in our lifetime we want to protect Nantucket because it's a really special place we've seen the best in Nantucket it's sad to see how much it's changed I think the old Nantucket is long gone my name is Susan Chase odesson I am a 10th Generation native nantucketer my name is Carl otisin and I'm a fourth generation native nantucketer we've been married 43 years oh hell no [Laughter] we're friends before we were born Carl and I were classmates forever and wasn't until our 10th class reunion we got together he says you go and I said yeah I guess I gotta go and he says so we went together figured there'd be no place to park if everybody took their own car so 196 1968 Parkin was not a real problem around here then growing up on Nantucket as a child was very easy to do foreign it was such a quiet place well growing up here was only 3 000 people you just had small groups of people that you hung out with we rode our bicycles we went places and picked berries and fishing and we may do it what we had and what we could grow in the garden growing his houses in the 1940s the property looked a lot different than what it does today farming was a way of life dairy cows horses Grayson in the meadow our family owned approximately 100 acres in which today covers from the rotary to Fairgrounds Road right to The Creeks the property we are on to today this property is very spectacular all the birds and wildlife it's crazy I mean it's just there's something out there all the time to watch Mother Nature just is a show in itself it's always changing it's always changing what's that um pink flower right there so is that uh where are you looking honey Lalo my mom and dad were married in 1940. my father was Albert k odeson my dad was a carpenter Builder very skilled woodworker he would make furniture homes boats so I would see him do those things I wanted to do those things and I spent the rest of my life trying to catch up to his skills which was no easy task yeah every aspect of building a house is something we did as a combined family effort my family is always taken a lot of pride in recycling materials and wood don't waste anything The Artisan family have always been into early recyclable stuff it's amazing how Mr otisin bad just would pick up a piece of wood and he would make a whirly gig he made his own nails when he built his boat I mean to me that's amazing the beam in the kitchen is the Mast from a ship that foundered off of Tucker Nook so we brought Jacks and rollers and we got it up so we could move it and push it down to the water's edge because it was a long ways from the water and we got it to float and we towed it back to madiquette and put it on a big trailer brought it home we did our own plow Moon we did our own design work and assembling it makes no difference whether it was a boat or a building or what have you The Sawmill on the property is something I put together from just mechanical pieces I had laying around I guess I always knew I wanted to build a sawmill it's the only family owned and operated Sawmill on the island people know that we like to have wood well sometimes they want to patch up a fence or a shot they don't want to put brand new boards up they want it to blend in where I guess the recycling unit for the island I mean we have a lot of very fussy customers and they don't want just everything brand new all the time so they come here this is our firewood supply for the coming winter Albert and I my brother here he uh we we run both our homes on on firewood furnaces all you need is a handful of newspaper and a match it's easy there's no cleanup you just use it step out and you're through solar hot water heater Outdoors for outside showers that's yourself box painted black with some black two inch holes in it heats the water up and I pushed the hot water out with cold water so the cold water is pushing the hot water into my shower stove we were doing these things before people become energy conscious it's a way of life on Nantucket when I graduated out of high school I didn't know what I was going to do with myself and I fell into a position of making Nantucket light chip baskets with somebody and he retired and I kept on doing it I've been doing it for 54 years it's fascinating I love working with my hands when Carl and I got married we created a workshop downstairs in the lower half of the house and we have a little retail area and and people would come and see actually baskets being made and the finished products were there and it became a in full-fledged business for us and everything is made right here from scratch in all the years that we've been doing we didn't never elaborate on who we're making baskets for so I finally kind of said from the White House on down and so I leave it at that I still make a few baskets for people that have had an order with me I'm trying to clean it up because I am retiring I don't put too much in here because the crows come and eat it so all right we're going to the pond well when I moved in back in 78 the bachelor pad that long ago yeah it was just a field of poison ivy and wild roses and I got itchy to get something going and preserve and open it up Susan expanded the lawn by about 900 percent when we first started I mean I had to go get books on what were the wildflowers growing here and my thought was to plant amongst the natural wildflowers that are already here some of the large willow trees on the east side of the lot I got the cuttings from damaged trees The Willows at the Harbor House had a we had a big storm and there were broken branches everywhere so Sue and I were riding around and I jumped out and I threw in a bunch of the branches that the crews hadn't picked up yet and I brought them home and stuck them in the ground various places around the lot they're down there now and they're they're probably over 30 feet tall they're 42 year old trees it would help to start it was the apple trees we wanted to get to those apple trees and see what else was around in the in the property Sue decided that we would get married under the one of the one of the closer apple trees so we made a path down to that and that was the beginning of paths this apple tree is at least 100 years old but this is the tree that we got married under in 1979 and we had just planted this rhododendron which was teeny weeny and it's huge now the early things that caught my interest in my wife was she invites me to dinner one night and she's cooking roast beef she put together a wonderful little dinner for the two of us and but she cooked that roast beef in a toaster oven with very minimal kitchen facilities she pulled that off flawlessly and I said to myself this is a very capable woman I worked on Yuzu Tommy of course he happened to come with a very beautiful home and a very beautiful view So I Married it we were approached way back in 2000 what were we going to do with the property we have no children so what are we going to do with this and and we hadn't really thought about it and this gentleman had some other partners and they were going to go in together and buy the whole eight Acres that we had they offered a surprise and right and we got that got us thinking wait a minute that's not the kind of thing we want to do we want to leave it for everybody not see it chopped up or hotel down along the road we just decided that we lived here for the rest of our natural lives and uh in the end it can go back to the people of Nantucket leaving this property in their hands it's going to show a lifestyle that we lived over there will bring a little joy to people like it has brought to us the otisin family

- Why this harbor is better than the rest! | Newport Harbor - Beginner's Pilot Guide: Kind: captions Language: en welcome aboard to Newport Beach California here is a beginner Pilot's Guide to the basics of Newport Beach focusing on the Harbor and surrounding area and remember land was created to provide a place for boats to visit so be sure to let me know in the comments which Port you would like me to highlight next Newport Beach is an Orange County Southern California just south of Los Angeles what is special about Newport Harbor is that it is considered one of the largest pleasure boating Harbors in the U.S this makes it a great destination for a visit or the perfect place to make your home Port the first recorded history of Newport Beach began when it was mapped by Juan Cabrillo in the 1540s however it took another 200 years before Europeans started to settle the area the history of modern Newport Harbor really started in the late 1800s when James McFadden saw value in the area and created a commercial Port where the Newport Pier is today this port was the largest business in Orange County at the time however it declined once the U.S government built the port of LA and San Pedro back in 1899. it was at this time developers started to come and dream of a residential newborn Harbor that's when the development really started the city was incorporated in 1906 the same year that construction finished on the Balboa Pavilion and Balboa Pier it was also at this time that the Pacific electric Railway extended into Newport Peninsula from Los Angeles to bring in tourism The Sandbar riddled Harbor was dredged and the islands that exist today were created from the sand and the silt Newport Harbor is made up of two sections the upper Newport Bay or Back Bay which is a nature preserve and the lower Newport Bay or Harbor area each of these regions are about three miles in length and are more or less divided by Pacific Coast Highway notice how Pacific Coast Highway moves Inland and does not cross over the Harbor's entrance this benefits Newport because a bridge does not constrict the height of the size of vessels able to enter the harbor this is a defining characteristic and why Newport is such a popular boating Harbor above Pacific Coast Highway is the Back Bay which used to be a water sports area without a speed limit today it is now a nature conservancy the area of the Back Bay just north of the highway has some boating resources but mainly serves as a transition from the Boating oriented lower Harbor to the nature area of the Upper Bay this section of Harbor is really only popular for boaters accessing the Newport Dunes launch ramp when transiting this area be sure to stay in between the channel markers the water in the Back Bay isn't fully dredged and it is a popular spot to run aground now onto the crown jewel of Newport Beach which is the lower Harbor newport's Harbor consists of eight Islands over its three mile long stretch of Waterway these islands and harbor are protected by an equally long stretch of peninsula which protects the Harbor from the Pacific Ocean something that makes Newport particularly unique is most of these land masses are filled with single family homes this limits the size of commercial marinas and in return preserves most of Newport Harbor's water for sailing and transiting boats here's a quick comparison of newport's Open Water compared to Marina del Rey and Los Angeles notice the difference in design and lack of large marinas in Newport Harbor instead Newport Harbor uses a mooring system with 12 different Mooring fields and two anchorages this allows for boats a sail between The Mooring fields and retains the water area of the harbor while also maintaining the ability for Newport to have around 14 000 boats these Mooring Fields should be transited carefully though because it is up to individuals to maintain their own Moorings these Moorings are one of the few hazards in Newport Harbor it is not uncommon for Moorings to have floating lines that drift and can foul a vessel's propeller for this reason it is important to stay in the marked channels unless familiar with the area another hazard in Newport Harbor is its water depth the depth of the water in the lower Harbor isn't a huge factor to most boaters however you can see by this survey that once you leave the federally marked Channel some areas near shore can become quite shallow continuing the discussion on hazards let's discuss the harbor entrance luckily newport's Harbor entrance is one of the safest due to its large West Jetty however this wasn't always the case it wasn't until the Army Corps of Engineers built the Jetties in the 1930s that Transit into the harbor became safe before this Surfers used to ride waves all the way into the harbor and while great for Surfers it was terrible for boaters the Surfers weren't entirely upset though because the creation of the West Jetty also created one of the most famous surf spots in the region the wedge the wedge is an accidental man-made surf break that occurs during large South swells waves bounce off the West Jetty and form with the way behind it to double in size this isn't particularly hazardous to boaters however boats have been known to miss the harbor entrance at night so be sure when entering in low visibility that you're between both Jetties the swells in the area are primarily from the Northwest funneling between Catalina Island and Palos Verdes in the summer months Southern swells make their way up from Mexico bringing with it warm water this is also the best time of year to go fishing the winds typically mimic the swells pattern blowing in from the Northwest in the afternoons onshore winds Peak around 15 to 20 knots on the stronger days in the fall season the area experiences what are known as Santa anas which are hot strong offshore winds which can reach above 30 knots while strong the terrain near the coast tends to keep the wind off the water and create some very flat sections near the coast on to the resources Newport Harbor has a wide variety of resources for boaters who are willing to pay for them due to the nature of the harbor being mainly residential homes dock space or Newport is typically double to Triple the cost of neighboring harbors this trend of being more expensive snowballs introduce about every aspect of Newport Harbor's boating resources however with that being said there are a plentiful amount of vendors who service both Newport and the surrounding SoCal region Newport Harbor itself has four shipyards two fuel docks and about a dozen yacht clubs if you're a member of a yacht club visiting Newport your best bet for a slip would be contacting some of the yacht clubs in the harbor transiting dockage in Newport is difficult to come by but the city understands this and they've been working towards making Newport a more favorable place to visit by boat if visiting you can stay at the city's new Marina Park facilities which offer a small amount of docks for up to 55-foot vessels these docks cost roughly 90 a night and on holiday weekends book up fast other options are renting a warrant from the city if you have a way to get to shore there are 12 public dinghy docks around the harbor and Moorings go for something like a dollar fifty a foot a night these public dinghy dogs are particularly useful because you can drop off and pick up on them but also many of them have space on the other side of the dock for you to leave your tender for a period of time all of this information and more is located on the city's website in the description below it's staying on board you can also anchor in one of the anchorages however you are not supposed to leave your vessel docking at one of the many yacht clubs is really the preferred location if you are a member of a club with reciprocal privileges Newport is also a great place to trailer In Your Vessel located north of Pacific Coast Highway is the Newport Dunes Marina this is a private launch ramp which does cost money however it offers amenities like boat storage and wash down stations oh and it's also the only option for a launch ramp in Newport Harbor but once the headache of docking is sorted out you want to have fun Newport Beach is Central in location to Laguna Beach Catalina and the Port of Long Beach of these three Laguna Beach is the best location for a day trip while visiting Newport you can anchor up in Emerald Bay which is a popular boating destination four miles down the coast inside Newport Harbor there are many bars and restaurants that offer Dock and dine experiences to boaters you can also Cruise the harbor which is a great way to spend an afternoon and will take approximately two hours to see the entire Harbor if you are a sailor you can also participate in weeknight regattas during the summer that sail up and back the length of newborn Harbor once on Shore you can walk around Balboa Island and see shops or head over to the peninsula where there are miles of boardwalks to travel as well as the Fun Zone the two piers are the main tourism zones of the peninsula and across from the Balboa up here is the Balboa Fun Zone this location has a ferris wheel and many great attractions for kids and adults like parasailing and whale watching here you can also take the Balboa Ferry which shuttles cars and people to and from Balboa Island if in town over Christmas you can also catch one of the biggest boat parades in the nation Wednesday to Sunday the week before Christmas Newport hosts a boat parade which has been going on for over 110 years everyone decorates their boats and around 100 vessels go around the harbor together this concludes everything there is to know about visiting Newport Harbor so if you enjoyed this pilot guide harbor tour be sure to let me know airport Harbor comes highly recommended to anybody considering a trip to Southern California and thank you for sailing with us

- No Welding! Flip Your Utility Trailer Axle Right: Kind: captions Language: en today i'm going to show you how to flip your axles without welding and without losing your trailer let me show you how now you might be wondering why can't i just flip my axle over and reuse the axle seats well on this trailer i've done exactly that you can see i've now put the springs on top of there reuse the spring seat looks pretty good right well actually not might be tough to tell but that axle's bent you see that little bow in the axle that's called the camber and that actually makes the tires not quite square the the road they're just a few degrees off and those axles are cambered just so that when the trailer is taking load that the wheels square up to the road put my square flush against the bottom it's out round about i have to say about a half inch by the top of the wheel and that means the inside of the wheel is going to wear faster than the outside this is called negative camber it can also cause problems steering and handling on the road so you definitely want to fix this by flipping the axles right over now usually what people will do is they'll just take the axle and they'll weld on a new spring seat underneath and rotate the axle 180 degrees from where it is right now and then i'll put the bow in the right position the bow should be pointing up and set it down as it is what i'm going My mom thinks everything's connected to other missing persons cases, like it's a big web. A lot of the times, she'll say things like, I know I sound crazy, but. It just started getting too out of control. I believe that Jessie was tortured for the drug adrenochrome. Adrenochrome is a chemical that our body produces when we are extremely fearful or have a lot of distress. It gives you strength, it gives you vitality. And the people that killed Jessie are harvesting that from other people. And when you drink it, it gives you euphoria and power. A gang member who told me later, that he had a video of her death. He told me that Jessie had been killed, that her body had been put into a barrel and set on fire. The police do have a part of that video. I believe that the police are protecting her killer. Okay, so let me see if I understand this right, you believe that there is a cult in the area and that because of the date and time and the seasons and the full moon, that whether that has any significance to anybody else or not, it does to this group. That is correct. I have talked to people that were there when Jessie was murdered. That said that there is that video and that that's the reason that they did do it. = Okay, you say you've talked to people that were there. Correct And have you turned those names over to the police? I have not cause I just recently got those names. Okay. So is it gang members, or is it a cult? It's a cult. But then you were saying that it's gang members as well. There's some people from every walk of life in there. Well I'm saying the theory, I haven't even heard this one before. This is the same theory. Well it's like, I feel like you always have a different one. That's cause you don't come and talk to me at home. There's a video of Jessie's death out running around all over town. They're showing it at parties, they think it's funny. I mean it's very, I had talked to several people. But, if you've so infiltrated this, why has everybody seen it but you? Not everybody's seen it. Very select people. You said it's all over town. It's being shown at parties, Well, yes. Cause they are showing it at parties, so. But yet you haven't seen it? No, I have not seen it myself. Now, the police captain also told us that, We have never had a case involving the drug adrenochrome. We've never even heard of it until Dr. Phil producers brought it to our attention. Now, the gang presence in Wenatchee is currently active but a very low boil. And they say there is no cult presence in Wenatchee area whatsoever.

- Inside The BLACK HAMPTONS...Real Estate EXCLUSIVE: Kind: captions Language: en In 1947, a Brooklyn teacher bought 64 acres of Hampton's beachfront for pocket change, only because white banks wouldn't let black families near the water. Fast forward to today, and those same modest cottages are selling for seven and eight figures, with some even topping $7.75 million. Welcome to the Black Hamptons, where families who were locked out of wealth are now sitting on some of the most valuable real estate on the eastern seabboard. Stick around because these stories of Azerest, Sag Harbor Hills, and Ninevea Beach are absolutely wild. Azerest, where it all started. Let's go back to 1947 because that's when this whole thing kicks off. And honestly, the origin story alone is worth the price of admission. A Brooklyn school teacher named Ma Terry, who went by Hunter, bought a 64 acre tract of land in Sag Harbor with her architect's sister, Amazed. They weren't just buying property for themselves. They were building something revolutionary. The first blackowned beach community in the Hamptons. And they did it at a time when Jim Crow wasn't just a southern problem. It was very much alive in New York. Quietly, but effectively, keeping black families off white beaches and out of vacation towns that didn't want them. Terry and Meredith subdivided that land and started selling lots to middle-class black professionals for about $1,000 each. That's doctors, lawyers, teachers, artists, people who had money and education but couldn't get a mortgage from a white bank to save their lives. So, the Azurist founders basically became the bank. They marketed directly to black buyers, kept the lot small and affordable, and watched as families started building modest postwar cottages along the bay. But here's the thing that gets me. Azerist wasn't trying to compete with [clears throat] the fancy Hampton's estates down the road. It wasn't about ego or status. It was about access. Black families just wanted a safe place to spend the summer, swim in the water, let their kids run around without worrying about being harassed or turned away. And Azerest gave them that. It became this refuge, this little pocket of freedom where you could breathe and just exist without constantly navigating racism. And the guest list absolutely stacked. We're talking Lena Horn, Harry Bellafonte, Duke Ellington, Tuskegee airmen like Rosco C. Brown Jr., civil rights lawyers, ambassadors, people who were shaping American culture and policy, all gathering in this quiet Bayside neighborhood to relax and connect. Azerest became more than just a summer retreat. It became a cultural hub, a place where black excellence congregated and thrived. Fast forward to today, and Azureest is still here. about a hundred cottages tucked along the bay, still overwhelmingly owned by black families, many of them third generation now. And here's where the story gets complicated in that way that real estate stories always do when money enters the chat. Sag Harbor's median household income is sitting around $154,000, which sounds nice until you realize the median home value is $1.52 million. Yeah, million with an M. And we're not talking sprawling estates here. The typical house in the Sands communities, that's Sag Harbor, Azurest, Ninevea, and Sag Harbor Hills combined, is barely 1,400 square ft. These are modest mid-century cottages sitting on land that's now worth an absolute fortune. A similar bayfront property in the area recently sold for $7.75 million. Let that sink in. 7 7 5 million for a house in a neighborhood that was specifically created because black people couldn't afford or weren't allowed to buy anywhere else. Now, here's where it gets tense. Starting in the 2000s, outside developers started circling Azureest like sharks smelling blood in the water. They began flooding the community with offers. big money, knock it down and build a McMansion kind of offers. And for families who bought lots for $1,000 back in the 40s and 50s or inherited them from parents and grandparents, those offers are tempting. We're talking life-changing money. But selling means losing the community. It means letting go of a piece of history that can't be recreated. And a lot of Azureest residents looked at those offers and said no. They organized. They fought back. And in 2019, the SAN's historic district, which includes Azurist, was placed on the National Register of Historic Places. That's a big deal. [music] It's recognition, protection, a shield against developers who want to erase the neighborhood's character and replace it with generic luxury. But let's face the brutal reality. Historic designation doesn't stop property taxes from climbing. It doesn't stop speculators from making offers. And it doesn't help the younger generations who want to inherit their family's cottage but can't afford the tax bill that comes with owning a million-doll property on a median income. Local reports say current Azurus properties won't sell for less than the mid $800,000. And most are going for well over a million. That's generational wealth, no question. Families who held on to land for 70 plus years are sitting on assets that have appreciated thousands of percent. But it's also trapped wealth for a lot of people. You can't access the equity without selling. And selling means losing the family home, the summer memories, the connections to a community that was built specifically for you when no one else would let you in. One longtime resident put it perfectly. We weathered the storm. And they did. But the storm isn't over. It's just changing shape. If Azerest is where the dream started, Sag Harbor Hills is where that dream hit its stride and where the money is now flowing so fast it's almost hard to keep up. Sag Harbor Hills, where legacy meets luxury. Sag Harbor Hills came next, platted around 1950 to 1952, right on the heels of Azeres success. Same developers, same vision, and same problem of white banks still refusing to lend to black buyers. So the Azurist syndicate, the organization managing these communities, stepped in again and provided mortgages directly. They laid out streets, maintained roads, secured private beach access, and basically built the infrastructure that made Sag Harbor Hills livable. And they did something really beautiful with the street names. They named them after African-Amean whailing pioneers and local black leaders. Paul Cuffy Drive, streets honoring the heritage that had been written out of mainstream Hampton's history. By the early 1960s, Sag Harbor Hills was filling up with families from Harlem, Brooklyn, and other urban centers who wanted that same safe summer escape. The houses were modest. Mid-century modern cottages, nothing flashy, but they were theirs, and that ownership mattered. These families were building equity, building community, building a legacy for their kids. Sag Harbor Hills also did something economically significant that doesn't get talked about enough. It helped save Sag Harbor's economy. After the whailing and ship building industries collapsed, the town was struggling. And then these black communities started generating tax revenue, supporting local businesses, keeping the village viable. It's one of those historical ironies. [music] Black families who were excluded from the broader Hampton's economy ended up propping up a town that didn't initially want them. But let's talk about what Sag Harbor Hills looks like today because the transformation is staggering. In 2017, a Bayfront house in Sag Harbor Hills sold for $7.75 million. The seller was B. Smith, legendary restaurant tour, TV host, style icon, who had filmed cooking segments there with guests like Oprah Winfrey and Artha Franklin stopping by. That sale shattered every prior record in the subdivision. It more than doubled previous sales. And brokers immediately said, "That's it. That's the new floor. You won't find anything in Sag Harbor Hills for less than $850,000 now." They were right. The typical Sag Harbor home is now valued around $2.16 million and the median listing price is over $3 million. Not sold price, listing price. That means sellers are confidently asking 3 million plus and expecting to get it. Here's where things get messy. Investors started flooding in. People with too much money and not enough respect for what Sag Harbors actually represents. They're buying up lots, combining parcels, tearing down the original cottages, and erecting multi,000 foot mansions that completely dwarf everything around them. It's the McMansion problem on steroids, and it's fundamentally altering the character of the neighborhood. CBS News did a piece on this, and the frustration from longtime residents is palpable. One local activist warned that without strict protections, you could see unlimited demolition of 300 plus homes, basically the complete eraser of the community's historic fabric. And the financial pressure is real. Property taxes are climbing. Maintenance costs are rising. Insurance is getting more expensive. Families who've been there for decades are being squeezed out, not by force, but by [music] economics. In response, local officials have been debating strict overlay rules and historic preservation measures. Surveys claim that 95% of San's residents support protections to maintain the village's character. That's an overwhelming mandate. But implementing those rules is a political fight, [music] and not everyone wants their property rights restricted, even if it means preserving history. Now, let's zoom out for a second and talk about the wealth dynamics here because they're genuinely mind-blowing. around $154,000. That's solid upper middle class money, especially outside of New York City. But the median home value is $1.52 million. That creates this weird tension where even high earners can't comfortably afford to buy into the market. You're talking about needing a 20% down payment, over $300,000 just to get in the door, plus mortgage payments that would stretch most six-f figureure incomes. Meanwhile, the original Sag Harbor Hills cottages average around 1,400 square ft. You're paying multi-million dollar prices for what is objectively a small house, but you're not paying for square footage. You're paying for location, bayfront access, private beach rights, proximity to Sag Harbor's marinas, restaurants, culture, the cache of saying you own property in the Hamptons. For the families who bought in during the ' 50s,60s, or 70s, this is generational wealth on a scale they probably never imagined. A lot of these folks are retired now and their homes, which they bought for maybe $10,000 or $15,000, are worth 200 times that amount. Their retirement nest eggs have ballooned into multi-million dollar assets. But for the younger generations trying to inherit those homes, it's brutal. Estate taxes, property taxes, maintenance costs, it all adds up. Some families are being forced to sell because they literally can't afford to keep the house even though it's worth millions on paper. And yet, despite all the pressure, despite the gentrification fears and the McMansion creep, Sag Harbor Hills is still drawing people in. The legacy matters. The history matters. Being part of one of the last remaining historically black beach communities in the nation is worth something that can't be quantified on a spreadsheet. Families are holding on. New professionals, often children or grandchildren of original residents, are buying in, determined to keep the community alive. The private beach access, the summer social scene, the fish fries and block parties, it's all still happening. Sag Harbor Hills isn't just surviving, it's thriving, even as it's changing. And that tension between preservation and evolution is what makes it so compelling. But if you think Sag Harbor Hills is exclusive, wait until you see what's happening in Nineveh Beach because this is where the story gets really intimate, really personal, and honestly, really emotional. Ninevea Beach, the last stand. Ninevea Beach is the quietest of the three, the smallest, the one that flies most under the radar. And maybe that's exactly why it matters so much. Nineveh was the third Sands development begun around 1952 just as Sag Harbor Hills was filling up. It's a collection of modest bayside bungalows tucked into a quiet corner of Sag Harbor Bay. And like its sister communities, it was created out of necessity. Black families couldn't buy anywhere else, so they built their own enclave. The Azurist Syndicate provided mortgages, laid out roads, maintained the only beach access paths. The Ninevea Beach Association managed a small private beach and boat moings for members. The streets were named after black veterans and Sag Harbor whalers, another nod to the erased history of black contributions to the region's economy and culture. By the late 1960s, Nineveh had evolved from a summer retreat into something deeper. Some families lived there year round. The neighborhood had its own church, its own social gatherings, its own rhythms. It wasn't just a place to vacation. It was home. Today, Ninevea Beach remains lower density and more residential than the areas around it. It's not flashy. There's no commercial district, no nightlife scene. It's just families, water, and quiet. But quiet doesn't mean safe from development pressure. If anything, Nineveh might be more vulnerable because it's smaller and less visible. Here's the preservation fight in a nutshell. The entire SANS area, including Nineveh, is on the National Register of Historic Places as of 2019. That's federal recognition, which is great for raising awareness and maybe unlocking some grant funding, but it doesn't provide local protection. It doesn't stop tearowns. It doesn't prevent lot mergers. And that's where the real threat lives. One local activist laid it out plainly. Without strict historic district rules at the village level, you're looking at unlimited demolition of 300 plus homes. Developers can swoop in, buy up adjacent lots, tear down the cottages, and build something massive and modern that has nothing to do with Ninevea's history or character. And once that starts happening at scale, you can't put the toothpaste back in the tube. Efforts are underway to designate Nineveh along with Azurest and Sag Harbor Hills as a local historic district with real teeth. Village reports claim over 95% of SN's homeowners support these measures. That's not a slim majority. That's near unonymity. People want to protect what they have even as they're being tempted by offers that could change their lives. Because let's be clear, the money being thrown around is obscene. Sag Harbor's median listing price is now over $3 million. Three million. For context, the median home price in the United States is around $400,000. Sag Harbor is more than 7 times that. And Ninevea's cottages, many of them under 1,500 ft, built in the 50s and 60s with basic materials, are sitting on lots worth multiple millions because of one thing, location. waterfront, bay access, deed beach rights, private moorings. In the Hamptons, that's liquid gold. Preservation Long Island has been sounding the alarm, warning that original Sands homes are demolished so their lots can be merged to build larger, more exclusive luxury properties affordable only to the wealthiest of buyers. Translation: The people moving in aren't the descendants of the families who built Nineveh. their hedge fund managers, tech executives, finance bros looking for a second or third home. And here's the painful irony. The same exclusion that created Nineveh Beach in the first place, the racism that forced black families to build their own community because they weren't welcome elsewhere, is now being replicated in reverse. The wealth required to buy into Nineveh today is so extreme that even successful black professionals are being priced out. But despite all of that, despite the pressure and the money and the existential threat, Nineveh Beach is still here. The families who've been coming back every summer for [music] 50, 60, 70 years are still showing up. The fish fries are still happening. The block parties on the sand, the family reunions, the kids splashing in the bay while grandparents watch from beach chairs. It's all still alive. For the descendants who still live or vacation in Nineveh, this place is a living link to a dream that seemed impossible in 1952. Owning waterfront property, building equity, creating a legacy, giving your kids and grandkids a place to come back to. And they're fighting like hell to protect it even as the market tries to swallow them whole. Sag Harbor Village has a population of about 2,450 people and only 3 to 5% of them are black. The rest are mostly white New Yorkers with Hampton's money. Old money, new money, finance money, real estate money. Ninevea Beach is one of the last remaining blackowned pockets in a market that has drawn global investors, celebrities, billionaires. The median household income in Sag Harbor is around $154,000, which again sounds great until you remember that homes are listed at $3 million. You can't bridge that gap with a salary. You need generational wealth. You need assets. You need to have bought in decades ago. Or you need someone in your family who did. And that's what makes Ninevea Beach so significant. The few dozen families who still own property there are sitting on multi-million dollar land. But for most of them, it's not an investment portfolio. It's not a retirement fund they plan to liquidate. It's family. It's history. It's the place where their parents or grandparents were finally able to own something when the rest of the world told them no. That emotional value doesn't show up on a Zillow listing, but it's real [music] and it's powerful and it's the reason people are still fighting to stay even when the financial logic says they should sell. Nineveh Beach represents something bigger than real estate or wealth metrics. It's a testament to resilience, to the lengths black families went to create spaces for themselves when exclusion was the default. And it's a reminder that generational wealth isn't just about money. It's about roots, identity, and the right to exist in spaces that were never meant for you. The mid 20th century dream of a black Hampton's wasn't about competing with white wealth. It was about carving out a corner of the world where black families could be safe, free, and unapologetically themselves. Azerest, Sag Harbor Hills, and Ninevea Beach did exactly that. [music] They built something that lasted, something that mattered, something worth protecting. And as land values skyrocket and developers circle and the market churns, the families still holding on are making a choice. Wealth isn't just what you own, it's what you refuse to let go of. And these were the three historic black neighborhoods of the Black Hamptons. Which one of these stories fascinated you the most? Let us know in the comments.
