- Cotuit and Horseshoe shoals part 3: Kind: captions Language: en Here is Cape Cod, Nantucket Sound. And Horseshoe Shaw is this horseshoe shaped shaw in the middle of horseshoe of Nantucket Sounds and it's open to the east and the weather station is located on the southeast tine corner of Horseshoe Shaw. So we literally are about halfway from Nantucket and halfway to Hyannis. Katouit Bay is over here. And we're sitting out on the tine of Horseshoe Shaw here. And we notice that the uh water towers are moving to the east because the current is pulling us to the west and we are pulled toward Falmouth and uh oak bluffs of Martha's Vineyard. Lacking an engine and with the tide taking us perpendicular to where we wish to go, we resorted to taking out from under the starboard bench a long wooden canoe paddle. We took turns with the paddle, pulling for shore while the other steered the boat. Each turn at paddling was up close and personal with a tranquil sea. Drawing the canoe paddle into the seawater, the pale brown ash blade darkens to mahogany color before disappearing into shallow depths. Brown water becomes opaque 16 in from the surface. This is disquing because this should be blue water. Instead, I am paddling offshore six miles south of Cape Cod, halfway to Nantucket Island in the middle of Nantucket Sound. And this water is mahogany brown on an ash paddle. The sea water color is the direct result of nutrients, including phosphorus and mostly nitrogen. Nutrients are taken up by algae. Benthic animals in the Antucken Sound, the bottom fauna, suffers. Slipper shells, moon snails, spider crabs, and horseshoe crabs all host slimy filamentous algae in long trails off their topsides. Nutrientfueled green slime is not a healthy environment for fish and marine life. Sea turtles pass through Nantucket Sound, but do not dwell here and are rarely seen. There are those who worry about the aesthetic of Nantucket Sound being destroyed by windmills. I now worry about Nantucket Sound destruction by nutrient overloading. The waters here are so brown that outstretched fingers disappear from sight before my elbow touches water. Nutrient overloading causes algo algae to bloom to reproduce rapidly. Sometimes the blooming algae consumes so much dissolved oxygen in seawater that organisms die. Water turns deadly brown, depleting even more oxygen when green slime decomposes into brown detritus. Devoid of oxygen, the area goes hip h devoid of oxygen, the area goes hypoxic and an ocean dead zone is created. Fortunately, I've yet to find an ocean dead zone in Massachusetts waters. However, I fear this will only be a matter of time and effort of looking for stressed out ocean areas. The loss of offshore blue began on the shore on Cape Cod in the islands. Much is being done by municipal and state governments to stem the flow of effluent from cesspools and septic systems. With each heavy rainfall, nutrients are washed directly from fields, lawns, and parking lots into waterways to the sea. More needs to be done to stem non-point source pollution with better buffer zones between lawns and pvious surfaces and waterways. Buffer zones are needed between the domestic and the wild. The coastal waters of Nantucket Sound are protected from dumping by the Massachusetts Ocean Sanctuary Act. Unfortunately, for my right hand at the paddle's throat here on Horseshoe Shoes, the waters are not protected. vessels are free to discharge effluent because these are federal waters circled by a threemile ribbon of state waters. So this is all state waters. Here in the middle, wild frontier middle vessels dump whatever they please. According to recent complaints, public ferry boats going to and fro the islands are doing just that. To their credit, some ferryboat operators such as the Steamship Authority are taking steps making the investments that will enable their vessels to cease discharging. To be in the middle of Nantucket Sound is to be in the nutrient overloaded vortex of the rich and famous, ringed by mega mcmans and traversed by overcarbonated Tupperware toys. Construction vessels will soon join the fry to build windmills on horseshoe shores. They like everyone else are free to throw their tea into this precious realm of ocean. How much nutrient loading can Nantucket Sound take? How much slimming of the sound, desecration of the deep before we act to reclaim blue waters once more? For too long, no one has spoken for the waters, for the ocean ecosystem so impacted and burdened by human waste. We can act to turn Nantucket Sound waters from brown to blue. Polluters will claim to not pollute is a hardship. They'll cry that this is government interfering with free enterprise. Great hardships are suffered by ocean users, the recreator and the worker. Yet, who is actually experiencing coastal waters when the poller is squawking of government-caused hardships? Being federal waters, Nantucket Sound belongs to everyone to desoil and to no one in particular to protect. These are our natural comments and without individuals speaking up, without environmental guardians, tragedies of the commons will continue. Being federal waters, these portions of Nantucket Sound can be protected from vessels discharging waste by legislation from Washington. And congressional representatives have told me that they would like to see Nantucket Sound so protected if only the local users can get it together. Together, we can clean the brown and bring back the blue of these ocean waters. If you would like to learn more about our efforts to improve oceans seamlessly linked to watersheds, visit us online at ocean river.org. Opt in to receive oie alerts or write to me at robot river.org. In this episode of the Ocean River Shields of Achilles, we have learned about a watershed very important to me. In the next episode, I'll be back in a different watershed talking with people who know it best. We'll discover what is being done to better and restore an ecosystem. By thinking locally and acting locally, we become more global in our stewardship of the planet. Until next time for Ocean River Shields of Achilles, thank you for sojourning with us in Kouet Bay and Nantucket Sound. I don't know. Maybe that's what they were doing. Heat. Heat.

Tara, before we present the plan, I thought that it'd be good if we went over a little bit about eel grass um and the status of the habitat within our harbors um and around the island. So, eelgrass is a species of seaggrass, underwater marine flowering plant. Um, it's a foundational species that plays an important role in Nantucket's marine ecosystem. It provides a host of ecosystem services including but not limiting limited to um it provides a three-dimensional plant structure um and a nursery for many different species. Um the roots and risomes uh stabilize the sediment and help um against erosion. uh it's able to sequester carbon and produce oxygen and it's really the key to the survival and the thriving um of the Nantucket base scallop. So most people associate I guess if you're from Nantucket you associate eelgrass with the Nantucket base scallop fishery. Um it really is an important link for this species of shellfish. Um it's part of the life cycle. Uh when the bay gobbs are young and in their laral stage and they go through metamorphosis, they are looking for something to attach to and that is eelgrass. And the eelgrass um not only helps those young juvenile uh shellfish escape predation because they're off the bottom, but the eelgrass also acts as a fence to kind of hold those scallops in the harbor. So, if we take a look back, um this graph is slightly dated. We're missing a few years, but um you can see the trend. Um the health of the fishery um was kind of at its heyday in the mid80s. Uh we are harvesting around 100,000 bushels and it's gone down since then. And last year, we harvested around 12,000. So, the fishery as it is right now um supports around 35 permits and active fishermen. Um, and that's because there's a decline in the habitat and it kind of affects the survival of the the scolops and there's also a lot of um human induced pressures going on on shore that have caused the decline of the the fishery. So, we do a benthic study every year um through the town in Nucket. And benthic really what that means is we're looking at things on the bottom. So, we're looking at shellfish, we're looking at eel grass, we're looking at sediment. Um, but it's a way for us to monitor the fishery and monitor the health of the eelgrass habitat. Uh, since we started our hatchery um shellfish production program in 2010, we've seen 130% increase in the total scallops in our surveys that we've been doing since 2006. Now 2023 was a really crazy astronomical year for Bay Scop recruitment and if we included that data into this percentage it would be 530% increase. Um so clearly you know the the restoration and shellfish enhancement program is working. Um, we just need to kind of focus on how we can help that uh, population kind of become sustainable so that we don't always have to add shellfish to our water. Can the bay sculp survive without eel grass? We know that they can. They'll recruit in sand, on golf balls, everything. But they really won't thrive. Um, they'll end up on shore. We have a lot of strandings in the winter. Um, and so we think there's, you know, a lot that we can do to help this fishery, uh, be sustainable. So, real quickly, um, we have a bunch of dives that we do every year, um, underwater. Our staff, uh, we're all certified divers. We have around 40 sites in Nantucket Harbor that we've been looking at since 2006. And we just started, um, looking at Madakut and Tucker Neck Island. So last year was the first time we surveyed that to just get a baseline of information. Um and so that kind of helps us, you know, assess everything from year to year to notice the changes. But we do have this long-term data set that's so important in seeing the trends. Um because it's hard to see the trend, you know, from year to year that the changes can be small, but over time, um you're able to to notice the big differences. So, for those of you that want to take a deep dive into what we do underwater, um we're looking at the total percentage of eelgrass coverage. Um we look at different types of macro algae, which is other types of um algae that's kind of covering the eelgrass that might affect it. Um there's several different species that we're monitoring in Nantucket Harbor. We're looking at the blade length of the eel grass and the fing scale. also fing are things that are growing on the eel grass blades. Uh we take a look at the sediment characteristics and how those are changing over time. Um a lot of Nantucket Harbor is sandy, but if we have a lot of macro algae um smothering the eelgrass, that sediment can become anorobic and it smells like sulfur and it's black. And so we're noticing some changes in the sediment as well. Uh we take pictures of the sites um each year. And then up above on the boat, we are also recording physical parameters which include cloud cover, wind, sea state, water depth, temperature, dissolved oxygen, salinity. And so this is done at the beginning of September each year so that we can be consistent with when we're doing this survey. So since 2006 in Nantucket Harbor um we measured 37% coverage of total coverage of eelgrass. And so that is you know combination of all of the 40 sites together. Some are super lush, have a lot of eelgrass, some are completely bare. And so that's what we've been tracking over time. in this year or last year in 2024 we're down to around 14% total coverage. Um so you can see you know we've lost almost half um since 2006. Um, one of the things that we're noticing though, and you know, might be a little bright spot here, is from 2020 to 2024, um, the rate of loss seems to have slowed. And so, we're hoping that that trend continues, and that could give us insight into whether our eelgrass beds are approaching stabilization. So, for Madakut Harbor, uh, we only have one year of data right now. Um we have about 12 sites total and what we found um this is based on acreage. So Tucker Island around there on the sites that we um surveyed had about 59% coverage. The eel grass is probably like four or five feet long. It's super pretty and just very lush. Um the outer part of Madakut Harbor um has a lot of water exchange. So that that yield grass is also looking fairly healthy and it has 53% coverage and then the inner harbor as you would expect um you know has challenges with nutrient inputs um that coverage is at 12%. So that's something that we're interested in you know going back to each September and seeing um how those numbers uh compare. And with that, I will uh pass this off to Alyssa to talk about the different types of eelgrass research that she's been doing on Nantucket. So now I'm going to provide you with an update on our eelgrass restoration efforts here in Nantucket Harbor. Um so we do know that eel grass has been declining since the 1990s. um there was about a 30% loss in Nantucket Harbor since 1995 and about a 24% loss in Madakut Harbor in that time period. Whereas Tucker Nuck and Mossia have been relatively stable. Um recent mapping efforts by the state of Massachusetts indicate that these declines are um the rate of decline is similar. We're seeing about a 1% decline um per year and as Tara mentioned um she's seeing declines as well. So to help compensate um or offset these losses, we've been working on um two eel grass restorations. The reason why we restore is to increase ecosystem services. As uh Tara mentioned, eelgrass um provides a number of economic services to Nantucket Harbor. Um the other reason why we restore is that it also helps to facilitate natural recovery. So you can help to jumpstart the process um es especially in an area that might be recruitment limited. Um it also raises public awareness and so people are more likely to support any efforts um to uh to protect this natural resource. However, there are a number of challenges. So even though we're restoring, we often say that you should be protecting first. And the reason for that is that eelgrass restorations are very very expensive. Um, in the state of Massachusetts, it costs anywhere between a 100,000 and 400,000 per acre. And then success rates are very variable. Most of the time, I'd say one out of 10 times you see a success. Um, and so again, you want to protect rather than restore. In Massachusetts, we use two methods. We use seeds and we also use vegetative shoots. Seeding is a new process that we're using up here. That's something that's often used down south in the Chesapeake Bay area. um down in North Carolina um even in Long Island, we usually use vegetative shoots and the reason for that is we have a lot of storm activity. Um and using vegetative shoots has been more successful historically. Um but I'll go into those methods in a few minutes. We just don't go out and restore. There's actually a process that takes place. So we just don't take seeds. We don't take vegetative plants and throw them out of sight. We actually spend a lot of time thinking about the stressors, what they are, identifying them, seeing if we can remove them at a site before we restore. We also spend time selecting a site in a system. So there might be a lot of eel grass in a system, but it doesn't mean that every little microhabitat is conducive for an eel grass restoration. So we need to figure out which locations in a system we want to restore first before going out there. We also spend time looking at donor populations and um the reason for that is you don't want to go out and take a reference bed, start harvesting seeds or shoots from it and have that bed already be declining or stressed itself. So we look for beds that have over 50% cover and we also um make sure they're greater than an acre. And then the last thing we do is we try to spread risks. And when we're transplanting, and what I mean by that is rather than transplant a half acre or quarter acre all at once, we do it over a series of years. And this is in case you have some stressor event come in such as a storm um so it doesn't wipe out everything at once. So we have two restoration sites here in Nantucket Harbor. We have one at Monoy and we have one at Fifth Bend. Um, Monoy used to have a lot of eel grass back in 2012. It was wiped out by a sholing event in 2013 and it didn't recover on its own. So in 2017 it was still devoid of eelgrass and so we started working in that area. Um the other site was Fifth Bend. Um in 2012 you can see that eelgrass extended along the shoreline. However, um 2013 145 15 it disappeared and in 2022 when we started um you can see what what the extent of the bed looked like at that point in time. So at both sites um we use the same transplant design. We laid out five transect lines which were 100 meters long and um we planted um a third of the transsect line each year. So in year 1 we did 0 through 33. Year 2 was 34-66. And then year three was um 67 to 100. Not only um did we close the exact the halfacre to um activities such as shell fishing and boating activity, we also had a buffer zone about an acre around each site. And this is what the design looks like um at both of the sites. So along the transsect line, we put out um 10 clumps of vegetative shoots. That's fully mature adult shoots. And um so there were five on one side of the transsect line and five on the other side of the transsect line. So you had about uh 50 shoots per meter. And then we put seeds in um the site as well. And so we either dispersed them putting 250 to about 500 seeds per meter. um they were either like physically dispersed or we put them in tea bags. And we use tea bags to help protect the plants from predation or the seeds. So this is just some pictures showing you what it looks like when we're working out in the field. So the top um left hand corner you can see a diver who is harvesting. Um, we use divers to harvest fully mature adult plants for transplantation. We also use divers to harvest reproductive shoots. Um, we then bring the shoots back to um the hatchery where we clean them and we prepare them to put them out in the field. Uh, for seeds we collect them in um June and July and then they go back to the hatchery where they sit until end of August, beginning of September. um when the seeds are fully released and then we bring them out to the field and you can see in the left um bottom left and right um where we're dispersing them. We put them in a test tube and you just disperse them on the transsect line or they're in the tea bags. All of our efforts have involved the community. We have them help us with harvesting. We have them help us with um cleaning the plants, processing the seeds and also putting in um helping with the transplanting efforts. This has been very important um for us as well as the volunteers. It really helps to um teach people about the value of this natural resource. They see how much effort is involved in restoration process. Um they also get to work with scientists, managers. They they just um there's a lot of benefits to everybody involved. Um in on our perspective, we like to use volunteers because it helps with project activation. And so if we need to um clean, you know, 500 shoots, that might take one individual um you know, 3 to four hours, but if you have 15 people, that can be done in about 15 minutes. And so that is, you know, one of the great benefits. So how do we assess success? Um we do it a number of ways. We look at we use drones to look at um the bed remotely and we also look at things in situ. Um so in situ the first year we go out we calculate the number of planting units that survive. So are they present or are they absent? But as soon as the bed establishes itself then we start looking at various plant parameters such as percent cover. that's the amount of area that's covered on the bottom. Uh we look at canopy height, that's how tall the shoots are. And then we also look at chute density. And then we look for reproductives. Um we want to identify reproductives in our restoration bed to make sure that the bed is actually expanding on its own. And so having seeds is very important. And so this is um monoy. So again, top is showing you in 2017 when we started what the area looked like. You can see along the five transect lines, there is nothing there. Um the yellow markers show you um the area that's closed to shell fishing and boating activity. And then on the bottom shows you what it looked like last week. So you can see that we have a lot of eel grass that's um that has been restored into the area, but we also have a lot of recruitment occurring. And we think the recruitment into the area is due to the actual closure of the site. And so if you have seeds that are naturally in the system and they're moving around, you have the area closed and the seeds can land into the area um and they can have a few years to establish themselves. And so we think this has been, you know, really the key to success at this particular location. Quantitatively, this is what it looks like. So I mentioned what percent cover looks like. Um as I mentioned we transplanted monoy over three years. Um so 0 to 33 was year 1, 34 to 66 was year 2 and 67 to 100 was year three. You can see the transc lines are all performing fairly similar. Some are doing better than others but um in general the medians are about the same. The red line uh denotes our reference bed which is Hudy Shaw and um so at Hussy Shaw we were seeing about uh 25 to 30% cover and so you can see that the restoration bed is fairly comparable. So we're we're happy with these results. Um in terms of canopy height um you can see that the transsect lines are all performing fairly similar. Again um the plants are a little bit smaller but that's okay. Okay, we're not too worried about that. Um, plants respond in height um due to light availability. If they're shorter, sometimes it means that they're getting um more than enough light. Um but again, we're happy. And then lastly, this is shootute density. So, um anything over 200 shoots per meter squared is considered excellent. Um and we have a range here at this this um in this bed um where many sections are over 200 per meter. And then this is a video just showing you what Monoy looks like. This video was taken last week as well. Um we have Caitlyn that is going down to along our transsect line. She's our eelgrass manager for this summer. And so you can see that there's a little bit of bare bottom, but then the bed becomes fairly dense. And this is what you see pretty much throughout the entire half acre and beyond. Um, you can see that there's periwinkles. Um, right there is a reproductive. So that's great. That was that long thin blade. We had some fish in there as well. And there's not a lot of macro algae at this site. So that's that's great as well. We have had some challenges and our challenges have been at Fifth Bend. Um so in 2022 we started our restoration at that site. Um we put out the same number of plants and seeds. Um and we planted 0 through 33 m on each of our transsect lines. We came back the following April and um the seeds had sprouted. That was great. They germinated. We had seedlings and about 90% of our vegetative shoots had survived which was excellent. Um May was doing the same. June there was a little bit of a decline. um July we lost everything and we lost everything due to spider crabs moving into the area. Um this was kind of like an anomaly. We don't often see this. Um usually spider crabs do not eat eel grass. We have issues with green crabs and hermit crabs but not spider crabs. So we decided to go back and we planted um 0 through 67 meters. So we tried to make up for everything that we lost. And we had the same thing happen again where by the following June, July, everything was wiped out. Um, at that site, not only did we have issues with the crabs, there were also some temperature issues. And this is not something that's um, new to Nantucket Harbor. We did a health study a couple years ago and we know that um a lot of the beds in Nantucket Harbor as well as Madakut Harbor are impaired um by um poor water quality in terms of you know excess nutrients that are coming in and causing macro algae blooms. So if you look at Nantucket Harbor or Madakut Harbor, you can see that we have um a lot of algae in the top two pictures compared to what you see out on Tucker and Moskegan. Um and then the as I had mentioned temperature being an issue um at the Fifth Ben site, you know, we were seeing temperatures um above 25° C. 25 degrees C is where um plants stop re they they're respiring. They're not photosynthesizing anymore. And then once you hit 28 degrees C um they can no longer survive. And so this is just a chart showing you from our health study that this is also common throughout Nantucket Harbor. And so um you know fortunately at Monoy we don't have this occurring but to expand into other locations this is something that we need to consider as we move forward. So what do we need to do to ensure success of our monoy site and any other potential restoration sites. Um Kim is going to go into this in further detail. But you know at our restoration site um you know it's important to keep those nutrient levels down. We don't want macro algae coming in. Um this can be done by reducing fertilizer, upgrading septic tanks. Um as I mentioned, the closure of the restoration site was very very important to helping the bed establish itself as well as grow. Um so this is something we need to consider in the future. Um conservation moorings are a concept that I think are going to be discussed a little bit more. Um, so rather than putting your boats um on traditional moorings that have the chain and tear up eel grass on the bottom, there are other alternatives out there to keep that chain off the bottom. And then lastly, I think the most important thing is um continuing outreach and education so that people appreciate the value of just eel grass in general so they're more likely to protect um what you have right now in the system. And then I know everybody always asks about um climate change. I keep on mentioning um you know temperature as an issue. Um if you start removing like the stressors that you can control you know such as closing off areas um putting in conservation moorings reducing nutrient loadings um then the plants have a better they're more able to fight you know the stressors of increasing temperatures. But I also wanted to mention that Nantucket is part of a study where we are trying to um identify populations that are thermal tolerant. And so um this is a collaboration um between Boston University, Stony Brook University, University of Maryland, and UNCC um Wilmington. And we're working in the national parks um just identifying which populations can adapt to temperature. And so hopefully at some point I can tell you whether or not we have resilient populations here and then what level of temperature we expect them to be able to deal with. So um that's all I have to say. Um you know as we all know eel grass is a very important resource. We know it's declining. We've attempted two halfacre restorations. Um one was very successful. Um we have over an acre and a half there and the other one failed. And um as we move forward, we need to continue to remember that the system is still challenged by light limitation and warming waters. And so it's going to be very important to um take appropriate actions to ensure that your populations are resilient to future stressors. And thank you. These are all the people, all of our sponsors, all of our partners. This has really been a community effort. So, I'd like to thank everybody here who's participated. So first off just talking about what is the purpose of the plan. Uh we went way back in time and tried to see what kind of historic conditions we could find um in terms of just aerial pictures of um eelgrass in the different harbors. We also wanted to really document the stressors to eelgrass to try and figure out a way to remove those stressors so eelgrass has the best chance of surviving. And then we identified recommendations and action items. So they're divided up into those different categories. um really focusing the plan mostly on protecting what the existing population of eelgrass is and then moving second into restoring. So how can you restore what used to be here before? And then um the other categories just talk about um research that could be done, mitigation that could be done again those laws and regulations which I talked about and if anything maybe needed to be updated and then the importance of outreach and education. And we were just talking about how important that is because it's really hard to enforce every single people that every single person that is coming into this harbor and talk to them about eelgrass and what they need to do to protect it. So really um you know education and outreach is very very important and then um collaboration and implementation. So how is this plan going to be implemented? This is um our tight timeline. Um and we basically started the plan development in February. In March, we had a Harbor Health Symposium, which I'll talk about in a minute, which was where we gathered a lot of really critical information for the plan. Um throughout this entire process, you can see on the bottom, we had a steering committee meeting every other week. We were conducting research, looking at papers, doing a literature review. We reviewed other eelgrass management plans which was really interesting to see what other people were doing both on the east coast but then also on the west coast and try and figure out what we liked out of these plans what um maybe we would have changed and they did help us kind of structure the plan and then again we received that really important input from the adviserss. So then we had a draft plan that was available for public review in uh mid June and so we had that up on the website received some feedback from the public and then integrated that and the plan is now complete and it's time to begin implementation. Um and so right now um you know that's that's kind of the next step in moving forward with this. And um the plan is a living document. So there can be changes to the plan as time goes on. Obviously the population may shift, stressors may shift. So it is something that um can be changed and edited as time uh continues on. And then here um we had the Harbor Health Symposium in late March of 2025 uh which I was at and it was a really great experience to collect as much information as possible. We had about 30 experts and stakeholders including a number of people in this room that were just talking about eelgrass providing information. We had roundts talking about all these different topics. Um this was uh funded by the Great Harbor Yach Club Foundation similar to the plan being funded by them. And um it was just a really great experience to talk about how water quality is affecting eelgrass, the different stressors, the importance of protection, uh research and restoration, and then again education and outreach. Um, so we just talked about how it was a marine flowering eelgrass, the importance that it plays in Antucket's ecosystems, and then um how much eelgrass has declined. And we've had a number of percentages that we've talked about here. Um this is just another one is um it's declined about 62% in Nantucket waters between 2006 and then 2014. We've had shellfish experts, water quality experts. Um, everyone coming together seem to think that, you know, protection is number one for us. We have eelgrass beds that are capable of recovery. We have eelgrass beds that are capable of reproducing and can expand and we just need to take care of them. And so that's kind of the theme of the eelgrass management plan throughout. Um some specific local recommendation examples for the recreational and comm commercial fisheries. Um we were talking about prohibiting clam raking in eelgrass beds. We all know that, you know, clams grow in eelgrass beds, but they also grow in sand. So, um, you know, clam rakes kind of, uh, rip up eelgrass. So, um, there are some towns that have prohibited raking in eelgrass beds, and maybe that's something we should consider, too. Uh, also from from the commercial standpoint, we've been talking a lot about this. Um, as we updated our shellfish regulations, um, we looked at everything and, you know, there are, you know, we are still dredging for scolops, which is, you know, something that we've done forever. Um, but just making sure that all the dredges are in compliance in the correct weight so that they can minimize any impact that they might have on the eelgrass. And so we were talking about the fact maybe we should have a pre-season inspection just to make sure that all the fishing gear is checked out and appropriate um for for the eelgrass habitat. One other thing that we were uh talking about for protection is the fact that maybe we should look at areas um that are more uh sensitive um and talk about rotational closures um and you know maybe they're this you know eelgrass beds are recovering or maybe they're being challenged but we certainly don't need to run dredges over you know eelgrass beds that are really struggling and so what does that look like to have a seasonal closure um just to see if there's improvement or something that we can monitor without it being additionally stressed. Um some boating and harbor activity recommendations. Um we were looking at uh we talked a lot about boat speeds and boat wakes. Um it seems, you know, we're right on Brandt Point at the hatchery, so we see a lot of the boats coming and going. You know, it's no secret that the fairies have increased over the past 10 years, the amount of fairies coming in, the frequency of their trips. Um, and there's also a lot of wake that gets kicked up from those fairies. Um, the no wake zone stops, you know, right as you come into the harbor at the end of the jetties, so kind of right off of the horseshed. Um, and you know, that creates a lot of turbulence to have that sort of wake right there. and it um caused turbidity that decreases the light penetration. It's affecting the eelgrass beds. So um talking about harbor speeds um you know as a as a way to you know combat the turbidity is you know something that came up as well. Um, we're also talked, you know, as an extension of boating, moorings came up, of course. Just So like the harbor plan um you know we talk about nutrient input and chemicals all the time. Um I think the eelgrass plan is just another lens to look at this in a different way. Um you know some people might not identify with water quality but maybe they identify with shellfish or eelgrass. So, um, you know, excess nutrients and chemicals in the harbor are a huge problem and it's one of the main reasons for the decline of of the habitat. Um, we haven't, to my knowledge, really looked at a lot of herbicides and pesticide use on Nantucket and how it is going into the harbor. Um, and so that was one thing that we were interested in, you know, starting to look at. Um, promoting native lawns and, you know, again, prohibiting the application of pesticides. Um, you know, finding a way to reach people and educate them so that this doesn't continue to happen. and then also take a look at our regulations and our enforcement situation and see if there are other opportunities to strengthen um that that so that we can find some way to limit what is going into the harbor. Um the research and monitoring um heading examples um we really had a hard time in developing what we thought was a comprehensive picture of the historic eelgrass conditions. you know, from those pictures that were black and white, it's kind of hard to tell if stuff is sand or ice. And um you know, there weren't a lot of aerials going on in 1930. And um so what we have is, you know, just kind of piecing things together and going through records and written records of what people might have observed. Um and then, you know, the aerials really started from um the DP, the state in the 90s. And so we're really trying to kind of piece that together. We're under no illusion that Nantucket Harbor had 100% eelgrass coverage. Like it's just not realistic. So, we're trying to see, you know, really how much we've lost. We have a few starting points from when we started doing surveys, but trying to really get a handle on that um will kind of help us know like how much we need to restore where would be an ideal level for us to get back to today. education and outreach recommendation examples. Um and additionally, you know, we had talked about having a ban um for anchoring in eelrass. Um and then we were talking about trying to find ways to incentivize um boat owners or people that have a commercial business on the water. And just to expand on what Alyssa started with, when we have been scheduling a illgrass transplant, we have regularly, especially if there's been a bit of a blow or a storm, sent volunteers out with coolers and literally filled them with the live eelgrass that still has its roots and ryomes attached on the beach. And we've taken that and we've actually used it in our transplanting efforts. Hi, thank you for putting on this uh seminar. It's very educational. Um why is the town not taking um restriction on fertilizer especially on the water front um more serious and at the state level what's going on and as far as title five and septic I know that it got to the cape but it didn't get to the islands when when will that happen >> I feel like that's a lot of questions for me all right so I'll try to go in reverse order to your questions there because they're a little bit simpler. So, um, title 5 is in effect on ant on Nucket. Um, so you do have to comply with that state regulation related to your septic system. One of the things that's gone a little bit further for both, um, Nantucket and Madakut Harbors in particular, uh, our board of health locally has determined those to be nitrogen sensitive areas and there are additional requirements in addition to Title 5 that need to be met. So, if you're within one of those areas, um, if you need to upgrade your septic system, and there are a number of triggers for those, I won't try to go through all of them because one I won't remember. Um, but I would encourage you to speak to our our local health department. They'd be able to answer that. You're required to go to an innovative alternative system or I system just for short. Um, and those when utilized correctly uh do reduce or have been shown to reduce nitrogen from uh the waste water that comes out of those systems and goes into your leech field. So, uh we could probably talk for 2 hours just about the functionality of IIA systems and septic systems. Uh so I won't bore you with that today. Uh but would definitely encourage you to reach out there. As far as fertilizer goes, um fertilizer is kind of the the the fly that we've never been able to really swat for for us at the town level. And um you know, I I'll say to this crowd that, you know, I I think the town and really all of us um could have done a better job to date in dealing with that and need to do a better job to date. I think that's a not only a town issue, but also a islandwide cultural issue for how people are managing their properties and engaging with their landscapers. Um, does the town have a a large slice of the pie in that? 100%. And it's something that we need to do better on. And I um don't want to give away stuff that's coming in the future here, but it's um major recommendations of both the harbor plan um the eelgrass management plan. it goes back to the shelfish management plan. Um, and some of those are there. There's been a lot of things around it with uh town meeting did request a islandwide fertilizer ban uh and sent that home petition up to the state. It's still sitting there with a lot of our other home rule petitions. Um, those go very quickly or very slowly. Um, and I think there's also a belief that a home rule petition to prohibit fertilizers on Nantucket uh will have a great difficulty in passing the state house for a a myriad of reasons. So, I won't go go through all of those as well, but um that is something where, you know, we we've had different levels of educational programs and outreach. They've worked to varying degrees. They haven't worked to varying degrees. Um, and again, to be frank, they they have not been good enough from our end. So, um, that's something that we are looking in the next, you know, 12 to 24 months to kind of revitalize and get back going. Um, but again, I know this is the wrong crowd to ask this to because you're here today. Um, I think you know we can look at the year 2023 and I wish I would have included some pictures in it because that was a really huge recruitment year um where we had uh base sculps just recruiting everywhere. They were in the sand like all over Winnit. There were like 50 on one eel grass blade um on golf balls on horseshoe crabs that were traveling by us while we were underwater. It was insane. Um, so I think you know this the harbor certainly can support a lot of shellfish. The problem was with that year is that they recruited everywhere in the shallow waters and there was not eel grass there to support them and keep them in the water. So as soon as October rolled around and the wind started blowing, they ended up on shore. And so we ended up rescuing 14 million seed that year either from shore or in the shallows. Um the shellfish association got all the scolopers out there helping um and we moved and relocated stuff you know all over Nantucket Harbor to deeper water not necessarily even ill grass beds just to keep it out of harm's way with risk. So I think that you know we can hand the harbor can handle recruitment and scolops. They provide filtration for the water. They help improve it. Um, we don't have enough fishermen anymore though to harvest what is out there. So, you know, this year we got 12,000 bushels. Our surveys told us that there were probably around more like 20,000 bushels out there that could have come out of the water. We just don't have that, you know, um, fishing pressure anymore like we did before. So, it's kind of like an interesting area to be in right now to know how much we need to put into our shellfish production. Um, because space sculps do die after 2 years and there are other shellfish that live and continue to filter. So, you know, we're trying to match our production with what, you know, the harbor can handle, but also with, you know, what the fishery can handle because there there's lots of other good work to be doing on the harbor for improvements besides bay scolops. And so we're having to diversify a bit with that. So hopefully that answered your question. I just have one um additional question. Anyone else? >> I I think that's a really good question for another reason, too, is I think as we're moving into the future and we're looking at areas especially like CO2, right? There's another question back there, R.J. I might send you for that walk. This was touched on a moment ago, but I'm wondering about storm water runoff, but not including fertilizer. What beyond fertilizer is uh a problem and how much of a problem start. >> All right, >> I'll get started on that one. So, when we look at our our nutrient inputs into the harbor and uh Tara or I know our water quality specialist Emma is here too, she can correct me when I when I get this totally wrong. But when we talk about managing our nutrient inputs to the harbor, we're really looking at three major pieces that we have kind of direct influence over. And we've talked about all of them. Now, we've gotten to storm water. So, we're typically looking at waste water. So, that's our septics and our sewers and how those are managing nutrients. um fertilizers and how they're contributing. But then also just overland runoff and storm water where you know when you really think about what happens when it rains, you know, you get a significant rain event, it hits the roads, it hits lawns, and whatever's on top of those roads, what'sever on top of those lawns is ultimately getting into our surface water bodies, it's getting into our groundwater, it's getting in in into the harbor. Um, and it's another area where frankly there's just we've started to the land and water council with our our storm water division of our sewer department has started to collect information on what the constituents of that storm water are and looking at it in more detail. Um, but working to manage those better. I think we're starting to see islandwide kind of a narrative shift in how we're managing those. I think we've seen a number of interesting little I know we always call them rain gardens and other parks, but they're they're essentially storm water filters that go in. There's a really great one on the corner of uh Eastn Street um used to be two North Beach Street. on East and Beach Street there uh that the land bank put in and its purpose is to take in that roadway runoff, put it in, let vegetated wetlands do what they do and take things out, put them into the plants and hopefully get cleaner water to either leave that area or filter down and get into groundwater a better product than what it went in. So, um we're seeing that. I think we're seeing that, you know, the local conservation commission started to address storm water a bit more on properties uh for how they're managing, but I also think it's something that with the town's commitment to storm water um really in the last couple years to inspect and maintain all those systems. I know we're actively working on developing a set of storm water regulations. Yes, I know it's just more rules and regulations to follow, but it will at least set a standard for everyone to hopefully follow so we can reduce that input as well because that's kind of the the third piece that needs to get tackled u as far as our major inputs. One more thing that I wanted to share with everyone before we wrap up um is something that I know we're pretty excited about and has been brought up uh a couple times this afternoon, which is our moorings um out in the harbor. Um, we managed to convince her a couple of years ago to, um, put some experimental alternative gear on our water keeper boat, which is mored out in Monoy, in an effort to see how well it would do at holding our boat during a storm and also uh, hopefully alleviate some of that damage and pressure to the surrounding eelgrass. And I might kick this over to R.J. just to talk a little bit more about what that gear looks like. But after 2 years and um surviving some good storms and and strong winds um it seems as though Sheila is Sheila Lucy the Nantk at harbor master is o very open to the idea of initiating some form of pilot program to expand that with a number of other vessels and we're just in the very early stages of discussing that. Um and we'll certainly be doing so with the town and and figure out what the best way is to move forward. But it's exciting to see the potential for some improvement with the types of mooring gear that we use because it really is such lowhanging fruit as far as uh preventing that damage and allowing eelgrass to restore in those scarred areas. So RJ, I don't know if you want to give just a quick overview of what the gear actually consists of that we've been using. >> Yeah. So um Sheila's very old school Coast Guard. So the traditional eco-friendly eelrass friendly mooring is a spiral anchor that goes a helix anchor it's called that goes into the bottom and then it's a big elastic band a big bungee cord to the moing ball. Sheila doesn't like the helix anchor. She doesn't think they'll get taken out. She thinks they'll stay in the bottom um over the course of scoloping season. A scolop dredge will snag it. That skull bow will go down. Somebody could get hurt. Somebody could die. So we have been working with her for two years now to find an alternative design. And the design that we are happy with is a pyramid anchor which then goes to the bungee which then goes to the moing ball and it has held well for a couple of years now. Uh our boat went on an adventure last October but it was for the traditional tackle on top. It had nothing to do with what's below. We care about eliminating the chain. We don't want the chain when the tide gets low to scour in circles and circles. And if you think about how many moorings there are, that adds up in square footage for eel grass that was going to grow there that will not be there because we scout it out. The good news is we get to keep our boats. congratulations on your 20th year sheila that's awesome okay so i am rg tarkov the nantucket water keeper uh that's a program under the nantucket land council as of 2020 so as a little background on the waterkeeper movement this started on the hudson river um when there was serious serious industrial pollution a group of blue-collar fishermen got together and formed what was then the first riverkeeper ever and their job was to be an advocate so the state didn't want to help out with these issues and these water quality issues these fishery issues the federal government didn't want to help out the local government didn't want to help out so a group of fishermen got together and basically acted as their own attorney general and brought cases forward and helped bring the hudson river back and that was the first one it led to what is now over 300 non-profit organizations from right here in nantucket all the way to the himalayas and the rivers that are flowing out of there so a keeper can be a riverkeeper a bay keeper a lake keeper really any body of water the thing that the alliance is created for is to have non-profits that work together to protect one thing and one thing only and that's clean water that's swimmable fishable drinkable water for everyone not just a few not just the people who can afford to live somewhere else everyone and it's really taken off as you can see on the slide we were the 354th waterkeeper group in 2020 there's over 45 countries i think every continent now except for the one way south uh over two and three quarter million miles of waterways protected and the north atlantic region now has 27 water keeper groups including ourselves over the nine states and 53 000 miles of waterways protected and we all these water keeper groups work together we meet every month and talk about these issues in our region every region has different issues the northeast has a lot of old infrastructure and a lot of legacy pollution they call it so our that little red rectangle on the slide is technically our jurisdiction the other light blue ones are cad render or gis renderings of the other watersheds that are protected ours isn't on the map yet so i just put a red rectangle with an arrow on it so you can see nantucket if you weren't familiar with where you are right now the next slide please okay so water keepers uh it sounds really fancy i don't have a badge i don't have a cool pair of aviator sunglasses i don't have any authority like sheila does or like a police officer what i am is the eyes and ears for the community so if you see an issue that you think needs to be addressed feel free to reach out give me a call send me an email and we'll find a way to address it they you just heard sheila rattle off all of the things that she has to do and all of the things she has to keep track of every year and she's great it's really amazing to be part of a water keeper group that doesn't have to have an adversarial relationship with the local government but she can't get to everything she can't see everything so that's where we come in we try to be eyes and ears out on the water and we can't do it without you we can't do it without members of the community who care and are out there and want to address some of the issues that are are going on all right so there's a few things that we are focusing on and specifically in nantucket harbor we're looking at eelgrass decline so eelgrass is a very very critical species a keystone species a nursery for almost all of our beloved fish crustaceans shellfish in our waters they are a carbon sink they can take wave energy and wave action away during storms and they are pretty critical and unfortunately up and down the east coast as well as all over the world we're losing seagrass so the water keeper program is focusing on the eelgrass and nantucket harbor as well as madikat harbor and trying to a monitor it and be trying to bring it back where we can and where it's possible invasive species are another one we have a very robust species of green crabs european green crabs here on nantucket they're very aggressive they compete not only with each other but with the local species they love to eat baby scallops they love soft shells clams and they love ripping eelgrass out of the bottom literally just ripping it straight out of the bottom so we're working to try to find a market for them and trying to find a use for them so that we can start to bring that population down and help out our local species another thing that we're focusing on is harmful algal blooms so this is something that seems to every year be getting worse and worse and every year we learn more and more about so 20 years ago there weren't too many people talking about harmful algal blooms on ponds or in marine ecosystems but now as the waters are getting warmer due to climate change and as we're learning more and more about these cyanobacteria species we're learning that they produce toxins we're learning that they out compete eel grass and other local species and they're a problem so we've been really focused on trying to address those issues and of course like every environmental group we are grappling with climate change and sea level rise and there are few places where it's more difficult to deal with than a community that is surrounded by the atlantic ocean so that is forefront on our minds and lastly on the slide we are addressing plastics as well so plastics were a great idea we invented them 100 years ago and we didn't really think about what was going to happen to them when we were finished with them and every single piece of plastic we made since then is still with us and we are working with the dpw nantucket dpw as well as umass boston to monitor our beaches take samples and figure out exactly how much plastic we're dealing with in our waters and on the beaches and that's sort of just the tip of the iceberg for a small non-profit but those are some things that this program is focusing on so this next slide is actually our jurisdiction so it's kind of hard to see but that blue outline which extends five nautical miles off of nantucket and goes all the way to hyannis that's our extra territorial jurisdiction as a water keeper group so any water resource issues or projects or anything like that that comes within that blue umbrella which is about 700 square miles we are the ones that the national water keeper alliance looks to to take the lead on it doesn't mean that we'll necessarily speak on every single thing going on in hyannis but as the group that is a nantucket water keeper we can our main jurisdiction is 131 square miles it consists of all the land nantucket has muskeget tuckernuck and one nautical mile extending offshore so that's our main jurisdiction that's where we do most of our restoration work our monitoring work um in our advocacy work so why do we care why do we spend so much time out on the water pulling green crabs out in pots and planting eelgrass a community like this our whole identity really is based on boats when unless you're flying here which good for you if you're not flying here you're taking a boat you're taking a ferry and nantucket harbor is the first thing you see when you come in and it's the last thing you see when you leave and most of the big events every year go on out there people from all over the world to anchor here and to come and explore downtown and see the whaling museum but it all starts on the water it all starts with our water resources and without them nantucket would be like any other town in massachusetts so we are working really hard to try and preserve our number one resource here and keep it for not only the economy but for future generations just to be able to enjoy and just be able to go fishing or wakeboarding or whatever it is that you're into take it away all right thanks rj so i just want to take a few minutes now to talk a little bit more about one of the primary aspects of our harbor resources that we've been focusing a lot on both the land council and the town i think the island as a whole which is our our eelgrass and eelgrass as rj mentioned is really a critical resource for a number of different reasons the list of ecosystem services that eelgrass provides to us is pretty long as he mentioned it really provides the structure out there for that habitat and for that environment i like to talk about it in reference to the trees of a rain forest if you think about all of the diversity the biodiversity that you have in a rainforest and then you imagine taking away the trees taking away that structure and habitat there's not a whole lot left of that ecosystem as far as support and that's really the role that eelgrass plays for us in our harbors it provides habitat for the nursery nursery for the fin fish also obviously for our shellfish certainly for our base scallops it also is a great resource for wave attenuation as rj mentioned during storms it really helps to buffer the shoreline from erosion and wave impacts and also when you have a healthy eelgrass meadow it helps to hold the sediment in place and really stabilize the harbor bottom similar to how beech grass does coastal dune it really has a similar impact on the bottom of our harbors and then finally there's more and more research going on that indicates that it's an incredibly important carbon sink for us these days and that's another thing that everyone's getting pretty excited about as far as trying to maintain the health of these habitats so we're really concerned about eelgrass because there are also an equally long list of threats to its ongoing health and stability some of these are natural threats that are beyond our control mother nature storms significant storms i'm sure we've all seen piles of healthy looking eelgrass washed up on the beach after a strong blow or some big waves and a big storm surge we also are dealing as rj said with climate change and warming water temperatures that's something that the land council's research has documented at this point that the eelgrass in nantucket harbor is thermally stressed and that's not likely to improve anytime soon so these are factors that are not necessarily directly in our control but there are others that that are in our control and that's really why we're here today in addition to nutrient pollution some of our land use practices which is something that this island and community has really gotten behind and focused on boating activities can also impact eelgrass and we talk a lot about water quality and the importance of water quality but there's also kind of a secondary impact of degraded water quality which is that the overall health of the eelgrass starts to decline so when you have activities such as fishing or anchoring or mooring that has a tendency to create a physical disturbance at one point in time that yield grass may have been very resilient to those disturbances and maybe could regenerate and grow back pretty easily but when you have a population of eelgrass that's really struggling to get enough light to photosynthesize it's not going to be as resilient anymore so water quality is important but we're also starting to look at some of these other physical impacts from boating activities and invasive species such as green crabs so we have been working a lot with the town in a pretty coordinated effort to monitor our eelgrass back in 2015 the land council worked to support the town's aerial flyover to do some monitoring of eelgrass and sort of a big picture approach looking at the distribution of the eelgrass beds and that's when we were able to document up to a 30 loss of the extent of the eelgrass in nantucket harbor we also have been working with some researchers out of boston university on a health assessment throughout nantucket at more of a regional look of the health of the eelgrass meadows and we moved that work over to madiket harbor last year as well the town has been continuing some long-term research on the harbor health looking at some different shellfish resources as well as the eelgrass and the vegetation in the harbor bottom looking a little bit more site-specific and localized and monitoring the health of these specific areas over time so it creates this three-tiered approach that really complements each other and long-term we're working to carry that forward so we can really document the health of the eelgrass into the future many of you are aware that the land council began a yale grass restoration project back in 2018 as an attempt to see if we could re-establish about a half acre of eelgrass off of second pier and monomoy this is an area where we know eelgrass had once existed and had been a pretty healthy meadow out there but we could see through aerial footage and also anecdotally had heard from some fishermen that after some significant nor'easters there really seemed to be some shoaling events that buried that area with sand and the eelgrass just never really recovered so we have been working to physically transplant eelgrass to that location the harbour masters helped us out a lot there by helping to close it off to anchoring and to shell fishing while we've been doing the restoration work it's been a pretty big collaborative effort we've had a lot of support from the town of nantucket natural resources department from anderson stillwater moorings the sunken ship and a whole lot of community volunteers who've come out to dive with us to physically do some underwater gardening so this process is basically removing some shoots of eelgrass from a really healthy eelgrass meadow hussey shoal out in the middle of the harbor and we really just break it off at the rhizome without disturbing the parent plant and we take those shoots and we bundle them together and we bring them over to monomoy to our transplant site and we literally work along transects and we dig holes and we plant bunches of these eelgrass shoots to let them re-establish over the years we've been looking at monitoring this area with survival in general things like shoot density percent cover the canopy height of the eelgrass that's there and the results have really been quite good 50 to 60 percent survival might not sound great but when it comes to this type of eelgrass transplant and restoration it's about as good as you can expect so we're really pretty pleased to see this level of success six and 12 months out one of the reasons that we suspect we're getting and achieving the success is because the area has been closed to anchoring and shellfishing and we think that it's helped to just enable it to recover both vegetatively and allowing seeds seed production restoration in there as well so i actually pulled a few stills from a monitoring video we have this white disc was used with a gopro camera under a boat in order to take a look at eelgrass density you can see just on the other side of the transect line just some small clumps of eelgrass that had just been planted this season and there's a couple more that's sort of what the area looks like right after we've been down there planting some eelgrass and then farther along the transect you start to get into areas that were planted 6 months 12 months 18 months before where these areas are starting to actually coalesce and vegetatively grow fill in with seed as well from the surrounding area and this is eventually what we want to happen to this entire half acre area and beyond so we are really pleased we're seeing a lot of this success and we're going to continue to work on that into the future we also are working with the town's natural resources department now to experiment with some seeding options as well we've been out and harvested some reproductive plants with seeds that the natural resources department is storing for us in a flow-through tank at the hatchery and when those are mature later in the season we'll be putting them out using a variety of techniques in the same area to experiment with actual seeding and it's important for us to play around with some of these different methods as tools in the toolbox so when we think about eelgrass impacts in different parts of the harbor we have a sense of what is available to us as a means to help to restore these areas in the future so with that i'm going to turn it back over to rj to talk a little bit more about our green boating campaign that the extraordinary way in the last 20 30 40 years they are not the black smoke belching oil leaking breaking down sinking scuttling old boats that we imagine that you see in jaws and movies like that these are very efficient very well made new machines that follow pretty strict regulations one of the biggest ones which i think sheila will touch on later is is switching to a four-stroke instead of a two-stroke motor for the outboards which is the majority of the motors out here and they've come a long way they really have but with so many in the harbor we still see environmental impacts and at this point it's getting down to the nitty-gritty it's getting down to the detail work it's getting down to properly maintaining your boat and making sure that it's running the way it was intended to when they built it because that's when you start to run into some of the environmental issues that cause problems for eelgrass and some of our other species so we started a campaign the water keeper program this spring through june just basically going over some of these really basic things that basically come with your owner's manual if you still have it so the first one is when you're cleaning it especially if it's on the water avoid using harsh solvents you can use vinegar and you be surprised how much you can get off just using a good old scrub brush and some water you can even use baking soda but you don't need to use anything too harsh or too stringent because that just runs straight off your boat into the harbor the next ones are paint for both the bottom and top sides of your boat so the old bottom paints used to be really toxic they used to have metals in them they're moving away from that they're coming up with formulas that do their job without actually being as toxic and they're a lot slicker and they prevent marine growth to grow on there so those are options they are expensive but it's pretty complex chemistry and they are worth it and then painting your top sides as well using a non-oil-based polyurethane paints that can hold up to uv damage is really helpful and it'll actually improve the longevity of your own boat this one basically if you're a sailor or even if you have a motorboat if you do not keep your boat bottom clean you are really really really going to kill the performance of your boat you're going to use more fuel and that's bad for the environment as well so keeping that bottom clean and keeping growth barnacles algae all that good stuff off it really helps not only for you to get where you're going and have a good time but to use less fuel which is good for the environment keeping your engine well tuned this one again these are very basic tune-ups every year allow you to use less fuel and run more effectively and make sure you don't have any leaks and gear oil going into the harbor things like that number six i believe so this is another one that unfortunately we have to tell people pretty frequently it's the fall of no wake zones so when you come into a constricted channel your wake can not only rock boats that are moored and sometimes cause them to break free but it can also cause calving of salt marshes and eroding of the shoreline so slowing down once you're into these no wake zones is pretty critical environmentally anchor responsibly this is a pretty easy one as well if you have the proper ground mission is the long-term preservation of Nantucket Sound and this is critical because the jurisdiction between state and federal waters is is unique in Nantucket Sound as you can see from this map you have state waters that extend out three miles from the shorelines of Cape Cod Martha's Vineyard and Nantucket and those are protected under state law called the Massachusetts ocean sanctuaries Act while the federal waters in the center are unprotected despite the fact that Nantucket Sound is one ecosystem so the alliance along with a large group of stakeholders is seeking protection for Nantucket Sound by designating it as a National Historic Landmark so we're pursuing two tracks to do that um Federal legislation and administrative action through the U.S department of the interior the federal legislation would not only designate Nantucket Sound as a National Historic Landmark but it would also ensure that the protections that exist under state law apply to the federal waters it
