- 2025 Nantucket Climate Change Summit 09.10.25: Kind: captions Language: en Good evening and welcome everyone. Welcome to the third annual climate change summit here on Nantucket. We're very excited that you are all here with us this evening. Uh we were hoping for some spectacular summer nice weather to be outside for our reception this evening but uh unfortunately it is Nantucket in September so we will be inside today. Um our theme this year is understanding local change and really getting a good community understanding of what is climate change here on Nantucket locally, regionally and and what does that mean for us. Um, we're really excited that this is the third year that we have done this program and it has sold out every single year that we've done it. So, we appreciate the fact that the community here on Nantucket is still willing and interested to show up and listen to this really important topic and and learn what we're talking about here today. Um, we have support from our funders from the Great Harbor Yach Club Foundation as well as the Community Foundation for Nantucket's Remain Nantucket Fund. Uh, and this program wouldn't be possible without uh that support and continued support into the future, which we're really excited about. Um, this program has been designed and hosted and put on by the Nantucket Conservation Foundation and by the Linda Nature Foundation. And our collaboration on this event is part of why it's so successful and why both of these organizations are really excited not just to be doing this for the third year but to be committing to cons continuing this conversation about climate change into the future. Uh our hosts today might as well introduce ourselves and we will be um speaking first this evening before being followed by our our guest speaker today. Um but I am Dr. Dr. Jen Carberg working with the Nantucket Conservation Foundation. And >> sorry. Hi everyone. Um I'm Dr. Sarah Boyce. I'm the director of research and conservation at the Lindauring Nature Foundation. So I echo Jen's welcome and thank you to our sponsors today. So why are we doing this for the third year and why are we committing to doing a summit like this into the future? Um, the purpose of this climate change summit that we're hosting is really to bring our community together to talk about the reality of what climate change is already here on island, the change that we've been seeing and what the impacts are, but also how we as a community can get together and respond to change. So, as an island in New England, we are uniquely positioned where we very dramatically feel the effects of a changing climate. We see it already in our dayto-day. Um, but as a community, we are also then positioned to be able to figure out how to adapt to these impacts of climate change and to be an example for other communities about how you respond and be resilient in the face of change. Nantucket is a place that has, whether we were doing it intentionally or knowing we were responding to changes on climate, has always adapted to change around us. It's part of the resiliency of this island, the sand spit that's out in the Atlantic Ocean. Um, and so getting together as a community to have a common language and discussion around understanding what climate change is because when we understand it as a community, we can then get together to discuss how do we respond to it? How do we adapt to it? It's a change that's coming. It's an inevitable change, but how we respond to it is not inevitable. We have that choice as a community. Um, and having this common understanding as a community means that we have a voice in being able to advocate for what we think are the adaptations that we should be doing. So, that's really kind of our driver in having this conversation um with everyone here. So, we again are excited and welcome you to join us in this conversation. So, I always like to start with seating everyone in the same language of what is climate change. And I'm going to preface this with saying this beginning part that I do about what is climate change, I'm going to shorten it down a little bit because our guest speaker today is also going to go into even more detail in depth um in ways that Sarah and I haven't before. So, we're really excited for that conversation. But climate change is long-term change and average weather patterns. you know what's happening globally, regionally, locally, temperature, precipitation, all these things that are driving this big global weather system. The change that happens over time is climate change. There's natural cycles of climate change that occur, but there's also anthropogenic climate change. And often when you hear the term climate change, that's what's being referred to is how us as humans are impacting what's going on in the world and causing systems to change in ways they wouldn't um except from our behaviors. And that's really directly linked to fossil fuel burning. Um you know, but also how we alter the land and use the land around us. This is a graph that I like to put up. It is the concentrations of carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere. And the last time I showed this graph last year, um that red line was shorter. We have had average changes based on the past few years of what that concentration of CO2 is in our atmosphere. Um that has increased. There are, as you can see, natural fluctuations and variations, but it has increased as a direct result of the burning of fossil fuels releasing carbon dioxide into our atmosphere. And as that happens, we get a change in how our system can handle that CO2 and what that does to the weather systems within the world. And you can see on one side normal CO2 versus the accelerated CO2 levels in the atmosphere that we're dealing with now. It's changing patterns of of heat and causing global warming to the system. and prefacing that with saying global warming doesn't mean it's always going to be hotter where you are. And that's what we'll get into some of those details both locally and on a broader scale. As you get a change in climate, as things as that global mean temperatures are changing on average levels, you get changes in all of your systems on Earth. And some of these impacts are what we're starting to see locally. Um, we might not have glaciers and ice sheets, but we're seeing changes in our sea level rise as there's changes um elsewhere within our ocean systems. We're getting changes in ocean temperatures, changes in rainfall patterns, drought patterns, seasonality. Uh, we're getting changes in temperature and the extremity of temperature. Not just things are warmer all the time, but we're having extremes in what those temperatures are. And then that has cascading impacts on all of the systems around us. on Nantucket and Sarah and I will get into a little bit of the specifics on a very local scale. climate change that we're seeing on island are these temperature changes in seasonality, precipitation, amount of timing, ocean warming, but the impacts that we're seeing day-to-day, storminess and flooding, erosion, which has always been an issue on island, but we're seeing acceleration in rates of erosion, sea level rise and associated flooding, groundwater rise and flooding, um changes in plant and wildlife ranges, changes in phenology, changes in how our ecosystem is functioning out here. Those are the details that we're going to get into for you. So when we talk about, you know, Jen set it set us up really well thinking of global change and global warming. And when we look at temperature, we know that while the we can look at global averages, we're really interested in our hyper local, right? So, like what's happening on Nantucket and I just like to think about um I want everyone to be thinking about what you're um reading, absorbing, listening to on the radio or watching on television, thinking about the numbers are going to always be, you know, you hear lots of different numbers and it's dependent on scale. also sometimes we're talking globally, sometimes we're talking nationally, we might be having state averages. And so kind of keeping that in your mindset too is really important for um you know kind of some of the information that we're digesting about this. So we know when we look nationally just looking at this map you can see there's a a heat map of colors and basically what this map is showing is the change um in relative air temperature since 1901. So the darker colors are where there is um a higher rate of temperature change in the warming realm. And so even without knowing the numbers, if you just look really quickly at this map, you can see in New England, we are warming at an accelerated rate. And in fact, you know, since the late 1970s, the US has warmed um more quickly than the planet as a whole. But then when we drill down into certain states and specifically New England, we see that air temperature has warmed by about 1.7 degrees CC. So that's roughly about 3° Fahrenheit. And so we're in New England specifically, but in Massachusetts, we are, you know, seeing a lot of these accelerated changes. And so it's a I hate to say it's a great place to study some of these effects, but it's, you know, we're scientists. It's always great to study things. Um, so, but we can see these effects um in our in real time. And this is what we're seeing already on Nucket. But of course, not only is it not uniform across the re the state and um and the region, it's not uniform seasonally either. So there's seasonal changes to these temperature differences. And so this is looking at um seasonal temperature anomalies by state. And um kind of the the quick and dirty of this is looking at our winter and summer temperatures because in Massachusetts um this is when we're seeing the higher temperatures seasonally. So, um, while we're warming, you know, kind of at a faster rate than other places in the country, our wintertime temperatures are getting that much warmer and our summers are getting hotter. And we really see that in, you know, um, less snowfall, less um, you know, um, not so much ice. I mean, how many how many times has the harbor frozen in the last couple of decades? Um, and so that we are actually seeing the effects of that those warming winters, but we're also seeing extended growing seasons. So there's lots of different changes that come about um as a result of these temperatures. And also we like to think about how does that feel to us, right? It's like maybe it's extended growing seasons, maybe it's um you know a milder winter. Um but I like this um infographic which came from the Massachusetts climate report. So of course it's you know Massachusetts centric. It uses really similar data, but it's showing that what our summertime temperatures feel like um the historic norm, which was maybe 81 degrees Fahrenheit. Our summer temperatures are starting to feel more and more like our southern states. So, by 2030, which isn't that far off, we're going to be more similar to New York. I'm really sorry, go Red Sox. But we are going to be more similar to New York. So places where groundwater is currently 2 feet below the soil surface will be wet, will be open water, will be a wetland in 30 to 50 years. So on the other side, the evaporation side, right, that hotter air forces more evaporation. So when it does start to dry out, it dries out more and faster. And that sets the stage for a lot of different things we see, including wildfires. And the biggest increase we're seeing for those fire weather days, and these are those days where it has the this doesn't mean all fire days, but when the conditions are there, it's the hot, dry, windy days when all of those pieces in the recipe come together. Thunderstorms. Now, severe thunderstorms has a specific definition in our meteorological world. It means a thunderstorm of you know very specific though 58 mileph winds or higher hail 1 inch or larger and or a tornado and the way we're experiencing severe weather still earlier research for the tornado side it's more that tornado outbreak days when we had multiple tornadoes on a day seem to be shifting and increasing. It doesn't mean more tornadoes overall, but when those conditions come together, they go. And also the seasonality and the geographic distribution. So, what used to be tornado alley is migrating east across the country. And what was tornado season in some of those areas, it's creeping in more into the spring and into the winter and into the fall. And so this is a changing risk conversation because if you're in Iowa or if you're in Oklahoma and you are used to a thunderstorm in May, you know what to do. You just intuitively react. If that happens in January, you do not react the same way. You do not go to the storm cellar the same way. And that's what we're starting to see. Now, also with the rest of the severe thunderstorm, some earlier research on the wind portion of it, and it seems to be that there's evidence that when we have a severe thunderstorm, wind gust, those damaging winds from severe storms, that there is an increase with a higher temperature. It's hard to get the data on hail right now. All signs are pointing to that there is more and larger hail. This is one of those where data, you know, is public or private. There's a lot of data out there in the insurance companies and we're seeing more and more insurance claims with hail, but we don't really have an analysis on that quite yet. So, that's just the atmosphere. The vast majority of the heat is going into our oceans. This the first time I saw this years ago, I was just blown away. 93% of our extra heat's going into the oceans. Only 7% in our air and all of those changes. So, this is playing out in some big ways, right? One being the hurricane side of this. Warmer water fuels storms. It leads to a storm growing to a higher intensity. It also leads to rapid intensification and that is another changing risk conversation because if you're in North Carolina and you've got a category 2 there, there are certain areas that you evacuate. If overnight that two becomes a five, your evacuation is very different. And some of those barrier islands take 72 hours to evacuate. So you can't just change that. So we're seeing more intense storms when they happen, not necessarily more storms and also that intensification happening more rapidly. When they do make landfall, they're coming with more rain as we saw with Helen and a higher storm surge. and they got into that piece of the sea level rise just going that much farther inland and that much more damage. And this is really building up the number of billion dollar disasters which is a high bar and this was well was all Noah data and it was adjusted for inflation. This data set was shut down. Now I'm going to shift a little bit to not just the general trends we know with our our changing weather, but some more specifics. And this is fun sciency, so we're going to get a little geeky here. Extreme event attribution science. Now general attribution is what developed in the 80s and 90s of how we could make those connections of climate change is is connected to these general trends. In 2003 there was a heat wave in Europe and the estimates really range from between 50 to 90,000 deaths. big one, really big. Um, and there were some scientists, Scott, Stone, Allen at Oxford that went in and analyzed this event and were able to tease out the role of climate change in that specific event. It has to do with our climate models, our historical data. And you can go in also in the models and play around with different layers and levels of carbon dioxide and how much can you recreate this? Can you recreate this? What does that look like? And you put all of this together and this is how you can tease out how much climate change affected an individual event. And the narrative used to be you can't attribute any one event. Well, that changed in 2004 when this came out. Um, so it was a 2003 heatwave change in 2004. And so since then, this has been a really rapidly advancing branch of science. The Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society was putting out special issues on this and they started in 2011 and they were doing annual issues, a collection of all these attribution reports that were popping up. World weather attribution still active today. We helped started a climate central. We've shifted our role and I'll get to that in a moment. But a a group of scientists that go into individual events they are putting out so many attribution analysis is is really amazing. You can go to their site and explore and they're global and they don't just get into made this event more likely this much stronger whatever it might be but they are also getting into the vulnerability the exposure of what is at risk. And then the national academy for those who don't follow along they put out this consensus report which is is big for the national academy is when science gets to a certain point they do this consensus report of where are we in the science what can we say where is it and sort of give that gold stamp they put this out in 2016 they said yes we can contribute these in these ways and this has actually continued to advance so quickly that they are doing a new consensus report right now because where this is playing out is not just in weather, not just in communication. It's also playing out in a lot of the litigation you're seeing with all the climate lawsuits that are popping up. This is one of the reasons. It's also playing out in the loss and damage conversation globally. And so, it's a really fascinating area to study. So, at Climate Essential, we don't do the litigation side, but there are some amazing people doing this. Well, I'll come back to the too honest comment in a moment, but let me start by saying that if you think you can sell a yacht to an ultra-high-net-worth individual in the same way that a car salesman persuades an 18-year-old to buy a Ford Mustang with go-fast stripes and low-profile tires, you won't be surprised to hear that you are mistaken. The kind of super-wealthy individuals that we deal with have a high degree of wealth, high intelligence, high ambitions, high drive, but a very low on how much time they have. And your job as a broker is to help them find the right yacht for them. It's really not to sell them a yacht. Your job is to listen and understand what they want. What will make them happy? What will be suitable for their family? To go out and find it, provide them with all the details they need to make a decision, everything from the engine hours to the history of the yacht. Is it in class? How much will it cost to add zero speed stabilizers or register it commercially? Will it fit under the bridge close to their home in Fort Lauderdale? The list goes on and on because your role is to be a problem-solver and resolving the problem of which yacht they should buy that's just the start of the challenges that you'll need to overcome. You will need to overcome so that you can save them the one thing they don't have a lot of and that is time. Now I know yacht brokers all over the world and honestly there is no one single personality that they have. Some are extroverts, some are very shy. Some spend a lot of time on the golf course with their clients. Some are first in the office and last out every day and yes some are very honest and some sadly are not. One thing is for sure there's plenty of space in the yacht brokerage industry for honest, hard working individuals of all personality types. So if you think you can't do it because of your personality type I'm here to tell you yes you can.

I think a lot of the um sexual assaults, a lot of the harassment, a lot of the complaints that crew have about captains and other crew member is alcoholrelated or drugreated. But ultimately, hiring a captain is at the discretion of the owner of a vessel or the vessel's management company. So, even if a captain has a criminal history, she explains that might not disqualify them. Somebody's making a decision at the end of the day as to how lenient they want to be. And while underway, the captain is the boss of the boat. So if there is an issue on board, wouldn't the captain be the person that the crew members can go to to report the issue? Yes. So what happens if the captain is the issue? Then they take it to the management company or to the owner directly or both. And that's only if the boat has a management company. If it's not used for charter, it may not have a management company. So there may not be anybody to report it to besides the owner who may not want to hear it.   4. @wlewis8336: "Ghost listings"—often referred to as "phantom listings"—in the yacht world are fraudulent or non-existent advertisements for yachts that appear on Multiple Listing Service (MLS) websites and brokerage portals. They are a form of phishing used by unscrupulous brokers to attract potential buyers with artificially low prices for vessels they do not actually have the authority to sell. 
YachtBuyer
YachtBuyer
 +1
Here is a breakdown of what ghost listings are and how they work:
The Tactic: A broker posts a listing for a desirable yacht at an incredibly competitive or "too good to be true" price.
The Bait-and-Switch: When a buyer inquires about the yacht, the broker informs them it is sold, under contract, or taken off the market. The broker then pivots to offering the buyer a different, legitimate, and often more expensive listing.
Misleading the Market: Ghost listings distort the market, making genuine, appropriately priced boats seem overvalued. They also make inventory look larger than it actually is.
Data Theft: Sometimes, these listings are used to secure personal contact information from buyers for future solicitation.
Stale Listings: In a more innocent—but still frustrating—scenario, a "ghost listing" is simply an old, expired listing for a boat that was sold long ago but never removed by the broker, often resulting from non-exclusive "open" listings. 
Reddit
Reddit
 +4
These listings are common on many brokerage sites, causing frustration for buyers and damaging the reputations of honest brokers. 
YachtBuyer
YachtBuyer
How to Identify Ghost Listings:
Price is too low: The vessel is priced significantly lower than similar models.
Stock Photos: The listing uses generic manufacturer photos rather than real, current photos of the boat.
No Central Agent: The boat is not listed with a reputable, single Central Agent.
Broker Unwillingness: The broker is vague about when the boat can be viewed or quickly pushes you to look at a different vesse

  14. @xvgwhalevvs.diamonds3281: Unless you're keeping it for 20 years, if you count mortgage as cost, more accurate to say monthly cost is $40,000, because the mortgage of $5k is the min - based on 20 years of payment, so for this to work in 3-5 years, really have to pay $32k a month on mortgage, and aim to just keep making maximum mortgage payments until it's paid(the only way to pay it off in a few years).

Your true profit is $32k a month in boat equity, until paid off fully, as should include the $5k based paid into the mortgage as profit(sort of) since it's not just an expense, each payment is gaining you equity in asset of the boat(I mean yes you technically own it and have full equity, but you know what I mean, as it's paid off it's converted from a debt liability to an actual liquid asset). Which, may translate to really like $20k a month profit in the end in real dollars(really profit after it's paid off and the boat is sold, I would look at it as working without profit until it is fully paid, the sooner it's paid the better, then the boat value is pure profit), as that $32k equity value is proportionate to the loan/purchase price so brand new price plus interest to gain the final asset of a few years used boat(which, isn't bad, not what I call "free", as requires years of work and upfront financial commitment, still $1m, just making it back). So yeah, in my eyes, $40k a month expenses, and a few years of break even, for maybe $600k at the end of 3 and half years, which really aint too bad, but still a grind/hustle to make it happen.

It's a good idea, though many people watching would get excited with the $40k coming in, pay the $5k mins, and pretend to already be reach, and really need to be paying on the boat for a full 20 years because they're spending $27k a month on bad bishes and blow with the "profit" instead of paying off the mortgage faster(which is why you have to be careful with calling that $27k "profit", since it's only profit if either you're happy with making those $5k payment for the full 20 years, or you're putting the $27k into the mortgage so you can realize that profit minus depreciation via sale of the boat when fully paid)

- Miami: The Don'ts of Visiting Miami, Florida: Kind: captions Language: es ¡Hola, viajeros! Soy Mark, de Wolters World. Hoy estamos en South Beach, en Miami Beach. Y hoy te diremos lo que no debes hacer al visitar Miami, porque no importa si vienes por un fin de semana, una despedida de soltero, o en un crucero, hay cosas que debes y no debes hacer cuando vienes a Miami. Y el primer "no" para cualquier persona que venga aquí NO TE OLVIDES DE VER LA CUENTA ANTES DE DAR PROPINA es "no te olvides de ver la cuenta cuando vas a un restaurante". ¿Por qué? Bueno, los costos de servicio ya pueden estar incluidos en tu cuenta. Cada lugar al que fuimos aquí en South Beach ya tenía incluido el veinte o el veintiuno por ciento, ya sea en bebidas, en un bar, en restaurantes, donde sea. Por eso, presta atención porque aun así, hay un lugar en la cuenta para que incluyas más propinas y tal vez agregues un cuarenta por ciento de propina sin darte cuenta. Así que presta atención. No pasa en todos lados, bueno, aquí en South Beach sí, pero estábamos en Downtown Miami en este genial restaurante peruano que no tenía costo de servicio incluido y tuvimos que agregarlo. Así que estate atento, ¿está bien? Y otra cosa importante cuando busques un servicio, NO ESPERES UN BUEN SERVICIO no esperes un gran servicio aquí. No voy a mentirte, algunas personas se me acercaron me encontré con dos estudiantes, Maggie, que se casará y Tiffany, y ambas me dijeron "Mark, por favor, menciona lo malo que es el servicio aquí en Miami" Tendrás que esperar, y no les importará si estás feliz o no con el servicio. No, no es así en todos lados, sucede mucho en South Beach. No sucede en todo Miami, pero debes saber que el servicio no va a sorprenderte aquí y quizás sea porque saben que el 20% de costo de servicio ya está incluido, pero es una cosa por la que debes estar alerta. Así que ten paciencia con el mesero, probablemente demorará en traer tu comida y tu bebida. Es Miami ¿qué otra cosa vas a hacer? Estamos aquí por la playa, por el sol, estamos aquí para relajarnos. Simplemente presta atención. Y como estoy en South Beach, creo que debería hablar sobre los otros "noes" que no debes hacer cuando sales, Primero, no reserves el servicio de mesa, (bottle service). En las fiestas y bares, NO RESERVES EL BOTTLE SERVICE ahorrarás mucho dinero pidiendo bebida por bebida en vez de pedir el servicio de mesa. Otra cosa que puede suceder es ir a un restaurante de South Beach o a un bar, y pedir una cerveza mediana, a lo que te responderán "hay promoción de dos por una en cerveza grande, o tres por una en estas de aquí" Y tú piensas "ah, puede ser un buen negocio, aceptaré" pero notarás que no dicen el precio. Así que mi "no" para ti es: No aceptes las promociones de dos por uno ni de tres por uno sin mirar el precio. NO CAIGAS EN PROMOCIONES DOS POR UNO Siempre miro el precio para estar seguro, porque una vez pedí una cerveza mediana y el mesero dijo "hay un especial de dos por una en cerveza grande" y pensé "tengo que probarlo". Y no estoy bromeando, por dos cervezas grandes, y eran grandes, reconoceré eso, aún con la promoción de dos por una pagué 45 dólares por dos cervezas con propinas e impuestos incluidos. Pero lo que es genial de esas promociones es que suelen traerte la cuenta y decir "¡mira cuánto has ahorrado, podrían haber sido 90 dólares y sólo pagaste 45!" Por dos cervezas... debes estar bromeando. No creas en promociones dos o tres por uno, asegúrate de mirar el menú para ver el precio real. Y otra cosa que sucede mucho aquí en South Beach durante la noche es que mucha gente piensa "no quiero gastar tanto en bebida, iré al almacén, a Wallgreens y compraré algunas bebidas y beberemos aquí" Mi "no" para eso es: NO INTENTES ENCONTRAR BEBIDAS EN SOUTH BEACH DESPUÉS DE 8PM No esperes comprar alcohol en South Beach después de las ocho de la noche. Quieren que gastes tu dinero en restaurantes y bares, A MENOS QUE SEA EN UN BAR O RESTAURANTE. y por eso cierran todo. Hay un almacén justo ahí en donde muchas personas me preguntaron: "¿dónde están las bebidas?" y respondí "mira, no venden bebidas en este almacén de South Beach, si continúas por ahí y doblas a la derecha hay un Walgreens con un anexo de bebidas pero está separado". Así que prepárate para eso, compra tus bebidas si quieres ahorrar dinero, pero hazlo antes de las ocho. El próximo "no" es algo que seguramente toda la gente de Miami quiere que diga y es: no creas que South Beach está realmente en Miami, está en el sudeste de Miami Beach. Tienes que atravesar este puente de Miami a Miami Beach para llegar a South Beach. No pienses que South Beach está técnicamente en Miami, si bien está en el área metropolitana de Miami, no te olvides de eso. Y también es importante que no creas que Miami es sólo South Beach, NO CREAS QUE SOUTH BEACH ES MIAMI esta ciudad tiene un montón de áreas diferentes para explorar como Little Havana, de la cual hablaremos más tarde, Calle Ocho, donde puedes ir a las fábricas y tiendas de cigarros, o a comer muy buena comida y beber y disfrutar mientras estés ahí. Pero hay mucho más que eso. Puedes ir a Coral Gables a hacer compras y comer, o a Key Biscayne, puedes alquilar un auto e ir a Everglades y hacer una excursión allí. Hay muchas cosas para hacer en Miami además de South Beach. Y creo que es muy importante mencionar que no pienses que Miami es un lugar alocado, con fiestas las 24 horas. NO CREAS QUE EN MIAMI HAY FIESTAS TODO EL TIEMPO No lo es, de hecho, es bastante relajado por la mañana en South Beach, esta parte de South Beach y Ocean Drive de noche es una locura, pero generalmente es en el fin de semana. Durante la semana, fuera de temporada es bastante tranquilo aquí, y de hecho si vas al sur, a 5th Street, encontrarás restaurantes muy tranquilos. Es como el día y la noche, Ocean Drive y la 5th Street. Y mientras paseas verás que Miami es una ciudad de verdad, y no sólo un destino turístico. En el centro de Miami encontrarás museos muy interesantes como el Museo de Ciencias Frost, el Museo de Arte Perez de Miami y Dade County, hay muchos lugares para visitar, como el planetario. Hay muchas cosas para hacer en el centro, y puedes ir a Brickell, a pasar un buen rato en uno de sus bares. No todo es fiesta, fiesta, fiesta, hay personas reales viviendo vidas reales y puedes verlo cuando sales de South Beach así que no te quedes sólo en South Beach, sal y explora más. Al pensar en fechas para venir a Miami, te lo diré ahora: No vengas en julio o agosto. El calor y la humedad son simplemente... NO VENGAS EN JULIO O AGOSTO ¡Es Florida! Everglades no está lejos, es como si estuvieras derritiéndote, y te sentirás así en julio o agosto. Y luego está la temporada de huracanes que va de julio a octubre o noviembre, algo por el estilo, es decir, hay épocas en las que no conviene venir, puedes planear tu viaje de diciembre a mayo, que es fantástico y el clima es genial, los restaurantes funcionan, hay cosas para hacer. Esa sería mi fecha favorita para venir, porque soy el tipo de hombre que puede llegar a morir con 40 grados allá afuera, así que estate atento. Otro "no" que tengo para ti, y que mi amigo Adrian, que es de aquí me dijo "Mark, por favor diles: no conduzcan en Miami NO CONDUZCAS EN MIAMI si no tienen que hacerlo, porque realmente apesta, y encontrar estacionamiento no es muy divertido, y verás que hay turistas paseando, lugareños yendo a trabajar, embotellamientos todo el tiempo, se vuelve muy frustrante. Puedes usar Uber y Lyft en toda la ciudad, es super simple. y son bastante rápidos no importa en qué parte estés, lo que me parece maravilloso. Otra cosa que sucede es que verás gente que llega aquí y alquila una Ferrari o un Muscle Car y presumen diciendo "¡mira, soy genial!" y conducen, algunos saben cómo, para otros es más complicado, así que mejor no conduzcas si no lo necesitas. Y si estás conduciendo hacia Miami, el siguiente "no" es: NO CREAS QUE EL VALET DEJARÁ TU AUTO EN UN LUGAR ELEGANTE No creas que el valet parking dejará tu auto en un lugar super especial. Porque la mayoría de las veces estacionarán tu auto en un estacionamiento normal. No irás a un estacionamiento secreto, irás a un estacionamiento municipal y te cobrarán el triple por el privilegio de estacionarte el auto. No diré que no uses el servicio de valet porque puede salvarte, pero mantente alerta. Y esto me lleva a otro "no": NO TE OLVIDES DE TOMAR MEDIDAS DE SEGURIDAD No te olvides de tus medidas de seguridad cuando estés en Miami, no dejes nada de valor dentro del auto, no dejes tus cosas sueltas en la playa, porque pueden desaparecer. Y si vas a salir de noche, no creas que puedes caminar veinte cuadras y que estará todo bien, toma un Uber, toma un Lyft, toma un taxi, así estás seguro. Y otro "no" sobre seguridad es: NO TE OLVIDES DE USAR BLOQUEADOR Y PROTECTOR SOLAR No te olvides de tu bloqueador y de tu protector solar todo el año. Y no solamente cuando vas a South Beach a las playas, si vas a pasear por Little Havana para comer, beber y disfrutar, también te quemarás, entonces asegúrate de usar mucho bloqueador, aplicarlo a menudo, usar sombrero, mantenerte a salvo del sol. El calor y la humedad se pueden soportar, pero el sol puede ser muy peligroso aquí. Y hablando de Little Havana, el próximo "no" es: NO TE PIERDAS LA CULTURA CUBANA No te pierdas la cultura cubana cuando estés aquí en Miami. Hay tantos restaurantes cubanos a los que puedes ir, SANDWICH DE CROQUETA tienen un sándwich de croqueta de Versailles con jamón, queso, mostaza, pepinillos, cerdo, pan cubano, ¡es fantástico, debes probarlo! SANDWICH CUBANO DE VERSAILLES Hay muchas otras comidas geniales aquí, y el sándwich de croqueta está bien, pero mi favorito es el lechón asado que es cerdo asado. ¡Oh, Dios mío, es fenomenal! Otra cosa que amo es vaca frita, que es una carne muy fina y muy buena. También puedes pedir tostones, o plátanos, ¡hay tanta comida aquí, no te la pierdas! ¿Hay comida cubana en toda la ciudad? Sí, puedes comprar en South Beach, probablemente más cara, pero buena pero te sugiero que vayas a Little Havana y come allí. Cuando estés en Little Havana, camina por la Calle Ocho y verás el Paseo de la Fama, verás personas jugando dominó como en la televisión y pensarás "es como en la televisión con Miami" y verás restaurantes, bares, verás el memorial de Bahía de los Cochinos y te recomiendo leer todas las placas para entender mejor a la comunidad. Hay un montón de lugares que te ayudarán a conocer más sobre la comunidad, visita las fábricas y tiendas de cigarros, aunque no fumes cigarros, como yo, que igual disfruto yendo porque aprendo cosas. Hay muchas cosas que puedes aprovechar de la cultura cubana aquí. Habiendo dicho todas las cosas cubanas que puedes hacer aquí, otro "no" es: NO CREAS QUE HAY SOLAMENTE CULTURA CUBANA no creas que todo es cultura cubana en Miami, hay mucha población haitiana y de Puerto Rico aquí, y hay muchas cosas para hacer. Puedes comer mofongos, puedes comer comida haitiana fantástica. Comí las mejores comidas tailandesas de mi vida aquí en Miami, en Coral Gables. ¡Comida vegana! Fui a Full Bloom Vegan y Chris, el chef ¡es fantástico! ¡hizo volar mi cabeza! Puedes comer muy bien y no creas que es sólo comida cubana, es comida muy buena de todas partes. El siguiente "no" en Miami es: NO TE PREOCUPES POR ENCAJAR. MIAMI ACEPTA A TODO EL MUNDO no te preocupes por encajar en Miami, Miami está hecho para todos. Tienes a las chicas doradas, las despedidas de solteros, los hombres gordos de mediana edad, que vienen a comer, beber y disfrutar, todo el mundo está en Miami, y puedes relajarte de verdad aquí. ¡Y eso lo que la hace genial! Es una ciudad grande donde puedes relajar, y eso es algo que me gusta mucho de aquí. Son felices recibiendo a cualquier persona que venga, y esa es una de las cosas lindas de Miami. Es muy acogedora, excepto por el atendimiento, lo cual suena tonto, pero lo entenderás cuando estés aquí. Y quiero terminar con una cosa que para muchas ciudades de Estados Unidos no es de mucha ayuda: NO OLVIDES QUE HAY TRANSPORTE PÚBLICO no olvides que en Miami hay mucho transporte público que puedes usar. Puedes tomar el tren en varios lugares pero si estás en diferentes barrios verás estos trolebuses en los que puedes recorrer el vecindario. Y muchas veces el transporte público ¡es gratis! entonces no necesitas pagar y puedes explorar un poco, porque si estás aquí en verano y el calor te está matando, ¿por qué no te subes al trolebús en Coral Gables, paseas un poco y te ahorras el sudor en las axilas? Así podrás disfrutar algunos mojitos más, más opciones fantásticas de comida mientras estés aquí. Ya sea que vengas aquí en un crucero, o de fiesta, o por trabajo, espero que esto te sirva para conocer algunas cosas que debes y no debes hacer en Miami. Les deseo lo mejor, y me despido desde South Beach.

Kennedy and Gerald Boore have all graced the deck of the Thunderbird yacht, a vessel that takes an entire crew to maneuver and a literal aircraft engine to power. >> Said he designed this after his DC2 Douglas airplane. Call it the hybrid. She's half plane, half boat. >> The Thunderbird is a custom luxury yacht built for millionaire George Wattell Jr. and its main purpose was to shuttle him and his guests to the Thunderbird Lodge, an east edge Tahoe mansion dubbed the castle in the sky. >> George would tell in the early30s bought from the California Nevada border all the way to Zephr Cove. It's 27 miles of lakefront property. Nearly all of Whatell's 40,000 acre property has since been sold or parcled out, but a crew of historians with the nonprofit Thunderbird Lake Tahoe Preservation Society now maintains the lakeside estate and retells all the wild stories. So, we're going to go down a staircase. If you take a tour of the property, you'll learn about George Jr., the eclectic Bay Area millionaire who inherited his family's fortune. So, the family made the money and and old Georgie, he uh he just spent it. >> Yes. Yes. >> Like a kid with a blank check, George added extravagant luxuries to his Tahoe estate, including waterfalls, fine furniture, an animal sanctuary, hidden tunnels, and a private poker cabin to gamble with other wealthy men of the era. >> Howard Hughes had a home across uh Crystal Bay, and Howard Hughes came over to play the game so he could match George Battel's bankroll. The Thunderbird yacht was purchased by George, but he didn't use it that often. Eventually, he sold it to casino owner Bill Hera, who apparently did have some good times with it. >> This boat has always been uh from the stories, a party boat. There's been a lot of consumption, food, and alcohol on board the motor vessel Thunderbird. One boat party that got out of hand involved some kids playing with the boat's controls, >> and they actually crashed into a dock, damaging the dock and uh and the vessel. >> Wild stories surrounding the Thunderbird Lodge continued well after George's death. >> There's an opium den. >> When's it? >> Now, we don't know who used it or how they used it. We just know it was an opium den. And today, the Preservation Society is always unearthing new stories to tell its guests. Riding the historic Thunderbird at Lake Tahoe. I'm John Bartell. Hope to see you on the back roads.

you're ready with all of your pre-trip preparations you have all of your safety gear your boat engine has been checked out and is fueled up you've got water you've got sunblock hats and stuff and your drain plug is definitely in your boat the most difficult place to operate your boat is at the marina or boat ramp currents tides breezes all those dang obstacles that you have to try to avoid banging into but once you get past there what else is there to know you see all these other people just cruising away in their boats it looks very easy but they've probably done this before maybe hundreds of times before we all think we can just zoom off in a boat but we wouldn't try that with a hand glider so what's a good plan when i bought my first boat a friend gave me some really good advice they told me to just do really short trips at slow speeds my first few times out and avoid water that's less than three feet deep like literally just go out for a 15-minute ride and then come back so that's exactly what i did i found a depth chart of the area i was going to be boating i plotted a rough idea of where i would go and what landmarks and markers i would use to help me i allowed for a little time to learn the controls and get a feel for how the boat operated and then i headed back now that might sound super lame and if you just plan to hang out on the lake all day in your new this might not completely apply to you but stick with me here i realized with that trip out that not only did i have some difficulty leaving the marina i got a bit confused recognizing right of way with other boaters and i learned that if i let go of the wheel the boat wouldn't even attempt to stay heading straight and coming back into the marina was even harder i took a similar approach to the way i did my first trip for my next several trips each time i might go out for a little longer or a little farther away i learned about the draft of my boat and how sometimes the charts can be misleading because of tides and shifting sands finding something like a submerged piling can severely damage your boat or your engine and suddenly put you in a situation where you have to remember how to signal for help on one of these early trips i shut my boat off just to relax a little bit float and drift i learned two huge things that day first i found out that my old two-stroke motor would flood if you started it incorrectly second i found out i knew nothing about how to drop an anchor so there i was cranking on my engine the wrong way smelling the gas fumes and drifting towards waters that i did not want to be boating in luckily i did end up getting it started and i got out of there and it wasn't a disaster but i realized that day that i still had an awful lot to learn before i should be venturing out very far with my boat so the reason to take short trips and stay close to the marina is to get more comfortable with operating your boat after about a dozen trips i actually was much better at every aspect of operating my boat i certainly wasn't good at some things yet but i was better at many things and much more comfortable with the process i've often said it's like when you first learned to drive a car you didn't take a two-hour trip into the city and drive over toll bridges on your first time out in your car and you should treat learning to drive a boat the same way short trips and build on your knowledge slowly i eventually mastered the technique to start and restart my engine under any conditions i learned how to trim my engine get my boat on plane how to cross the wake from a bigger boat without scaring my passengers basically i became a lot more experienced but if i had just headed out to some far-off destination on my very first trip out it could have easily ended in disaster especially if i was to naively assume that driving a boat is easy and no different than driving a car because i was cautious and took baby steps and also because i had a very   1. @mrunning10 [1 likes]: All of this is good, EXCEPT one thing:  don't buy until YOU have a place to park that floating hunk of fiberglass.  Period.  Or you WILL be sorry.  And I mean don't just call around.  Put that deposit down on that slip or mooring ball BEFORE.
  2. @BobbyArrow [1 likes]: Enjoyed the video. Regarding the "checklist" the first question any sailor in Europe will ask is "what is your program?" Do they want to do the Med? Cross oceans? First define the program and then, as you say, just buy something that can get you started on your program (provided you can convince your partner😞).

They became USCG licensed captains. They scraped by. There were dock fees, mechanical breakdowns, and nights when leaks made promises feel fragile. But the boat gave them something else. Identity, belonging, and a truth no apartment or Hollywood audition could offer. Then came the cameras. They started with yacht walkthroughs, travel videos, and glimpses of life aboard. A single video leaked into viral territory. A yacht tour that caught people's imaginations. What does it feel like to step into someone else's dream? Who hasn't wondered what it would be like to open a door to light hitting polished teak and chrome? See the sunrise over bow rails and smell salt and varnish. The audience held its breath and nautiles became more than a channel. They became confessionals, inspiration and aspiration. They made a promise. No payments for tours and no secret deals with shipyards offering yachts in exchange for good words. They wanted to preserve honesty. Victoria says they only tour vessels they believe will fascinate their audience, not just push product. But that promise came with Cartage saying no means passing up big money slinking through months without expecting to make a profit. They are very real people behind the glossier uploads or long hours hunched over editing, budgeting, and overnegotiating shipyards. Sometimes the steel hall doesn't align right. Sometimes the subcontractors deliver parts late or wrong. And sometimes the buyer wonders if this dream was too big. The Bearing 75, which they signed on to with Bearing Yachts, Turkey, Bulgaria, as a semi-custom steel explorer, is gargantuan. 23.4 44 m, over 160 metric tons, range of 4,000 plus nautical miles. Your audience expects perfection, forward motion, new boats, and new finishes. But projects sputter. The Bearing 75 build has been public for years. Many viewers expect pristine updates. But boat building is messy. Steel doesn't always weld perfectly. Paint doesn't always cure cleanly. Parts rust. Costs double. And days of sunshine yield to storms. There are logistical nightmares. Shipyards in foreign countries. Language barriers. Supply chains disrupted and material cost inflation. Steel labor transport. It all jumps. What looked affordable in early designs can become impossible in later revisions. Once building the B75, they must have felt the tension between the dream hall and the price tag creeping up. If delays happen, a shipyard might prioritize other clients. If parts are wrong, they wait weeks. Every day of delay drags expenses. On top of that, living aboard while building something new means your home is always under construction. Not just physically, but emotionally. When you film almost everything, the small fights over design choices become public, even if unspoken. You can't slip away and quietly decide, "No, we don't want that furniture finished." If you change your mind, viewers expect an explanation. There is a tension between being brand managers and spouses and also sometimes between love and survival. And there have been whispers, not scandals, but shadows. Forums mention concerns about the choice of builder. Some boat yaching forums talk about bearings, concerns over quality, whether the hulls are heavy, stabilizers are ideal, or whether cost cutting may compromise performance. The audience often doesn't know when they are watching hope. When they see shiny woods and polished surfaces, what they may not see is plywood backing that cracked. It comes from marine business, dealership, and consulting. But all of that multiplies the work and multiplies the vulnerability. Clients expect results. Contractors demand payment. Leave it&nbsp; to Joe Lewis to ensure he can practice his&nbsp;&nbsp; backhand while afloat. Combined&nbsp;&nbsp; with lavish furnishings and fixtures, Aviva&nbsp; feels like a floating private museum. Yes,&nbsp;&nbsp; she offers rarities and indulgences&nbsp; beyond imagination's reach for most. Aviva is crewed by up to 17 staff ready&nbsp; to cater to Joe Lewis's smallest fancy 24&nbsp;&nbsp; hours a day. With a top speed around 17&nbsp; knots from her hardy MTU diesel engines,&nbsp;&nbsp; his home away from home can whisk Lewis and&nbsp; friends to any tropical destination swiftly. you're you know not really into the hustle and bustle of Charter but trust me chartering although it can be so difficult it is really rewarding and the money is awesome so speaking of money let's talk about pay the yachting industry pays so well that I wish I didn't go to college like legitimately it's surprisingly good Google will tell you that the average yacht salary for a junior crew is 3,000 to 3500 a month but if you get on a good boat it can be a lot more it should be said that it depends on where the boat is if you are yachting in Australia you're not going to get paid as much as if you are yachting in the Mediterranean or the Caribbean it's just a fact my starting salary as a junior deckhand with no experience was 4500 a month with a promise that every 6 months we would get a $500 raise so after a year it was 5,500 a month additionally chter tips are usually about 20% of the charter price of the yacht split evenly throughout the crew that means the deck hand and the stewards get the exact same share as the captain the chief stew the first mate to give an example I once did a 12-day charter in St Barts and everyone on the crew was tipped1 13,300 and we were all given a gift Midway through the chter I was literally told to run ashore and spend $3,500 as quickly as I could as a Christmas gift from the chter guest this was tiered so the chief stew and the first officer got way bigger gifts than me but it was still pretty awesome I got a Caribbean exclusive tag watch by the way and the chief officer got got a lot of Louis Vuitton it was freaking awesome so safe to say the pay is pretty good not to mention you have no living expenses if you're smart all of your food comes to you and it's like steak and lobster and you have no rent you have no car payment you can literally live for free and save 100% of your income if you wanted to many people can go into the yadaan industry travel and legitimately save away $100,000 in just one year so it doesn't It is now around 11am on April 9th, 2025. The Port of Miami's MSC Cruise Terminal AA officially opened on April 5, 2025. At approximately 40,000 square meters (490,000 square feet), it is the world's largest cruise terminal. Let's go inside. Of course, since it's a new terminal, the inside is clean. A video of the MSC Cruise Terminal at the Port of Miami will be played. Construction was began in March 2022. At 492,000 square feet, it is considered the largest cruise terminal in the world. MSC World America is heading to MSC Cruises' private island. The name of MSC Cruises' private island is Ocean Cay. The island is located 105 km (65 miles) from the Port of Miami. The island is located in the Bimini area of the Bahamas. This island is an artificial island, created by dredging by a Hawaiian company in the late 1960s. This artificial island was used to mine white oval aragonite sand for various industrial uses. The total area of the island is 42 hectares. The island had been abandoned by a Hawaiian company and was in a state of disrepair. In 2015, MSC Cruises signed a 100-year lease with the Bahamian government and began redeveloping the island. The redevelopment cost is estimated to be approximately $500 million. This private island from MSC Cruises officially opened on December 5, 2019. Arrived around 8:00 am. Cabins are cleaned once or twice a day (depending on cabin rank). On the bed... A piece of paper asking you to watch the safety video. It has been thoroughly cleaned. factory in Normandy and this one is is very simple the motion the way it works here is you have uh this membrane this is the only moving piece here through a series of electromagnets that vibrate back and forth um and it vibrates this rubber piece drawing water in from the sides shooting it out over the the cone opening here and then that creates a a difference in pressures between this opening and the other side of the pot so more water will follow that way it's basically principle all right and let it rip this is the front mot the front yep and then this would be the back here but the motor itself rotates 360 so you can direct the thrust in any direction since you don't have a reverse like a traditional propeller um then basically the the main advantage is that you can you can touch it something like Freedom Boat Club they are a club with a lot of locations in the US Canada and even France over 300 locations means it's important to make sure one is near you but also there could be locations where you might plan to travel my main Home Port for Freedom Boat Club would be just across my river and they also have locations in Baltimore and Annapolis and even Washington DC how cool is that like I could go to Washington DC and take a boat out for the day now I know from here for me to go up to Baltimore is a couple hour of boat rides so that's quite a bit of fuel just for me to be able to boat around Baltimore however with them having a location there as a member if I wanted to I could reserve a boat in Baltimore and take that boat out for the afternoon and if you're traveling for work or for vacation as a member of Freedom Boat Club you also have limited access to boats at any other location so for example San Diego Miami Chicago whatever and if you've ever dealt with or thought about the challenges of trailering your own boat to a vacation destination wouldn't the vacation be much easier if you could just hop in a boat when you're at your destination and all you have to pay for is the fuel that's a pretty huge thing if you ask me but as with anything there are also some negatives Freedom Boat Club is not for everyone because paying x amount of dollars per month all the time to have access to boats for some people is simply not in their budget and they definitely do have some restrictions with regards to the usage of their boats for example you can't use their boats after Sundown or Before Dawn and as I mentioned earlier they only have so many boats per location for some people that's going to be a deal breaker and I get that it's also not for you if you just plan to go out fishing periodically in a small boat because you can just buy a small boat a whole lot cheaper than it would be belonging to Freedom Boat Club plus you can take it out before the sun comes up and be out there catching the fish early in the morning and if you're someone like me where my Marina is literally just a block from my house my marina fees are negligible I can store my boat in my yard and I use older boats that do need a lot of work unfortunately but my yearly costs are maybe $100 a month and that's much less than what I would spend belonging to Freedom Boat Club and because I have water access and my boat is a block away I can literally walk down to my boat after dinner and within 5 minutes be out on the water if I was a member of Freedom Boat Club my nearest Freedom Boat Club location is over a half an hour drive away any time I wanted to take a boat out I would have to be planning ahead to drive somewhere I can't be nearly as spontaneous as I - Are Miami Yacht Charters Too Expensive? - Everything About Miami: Kind: captions Language: en Are Miami yacht charters too expensive? Imagine cruising through Miami's sparkling waters, feeling the warm sun on your face and the breeze in your hair. That's what a yacht charter offers. But many wonder if it's out of reach financially. Well, let's break down the costs so you can see what's involved. Yacht charters in Miami can range widely in price. Smaller boats or basic rentals usually start around $100 to $200 per hour. These are perfect for a quick trip or a small group. If you want something more luxurious, expect to pay several,000 for a half day or full day charter. High-end yachts often start at around $5,000 for a day and can go much higher depending on size and amenities. For a full week on a luxury yacht, prices can range from 20,000 to $100,000 or more. These yachts come with professional crews, safety gear, and extras like catering, music, or water sports equipment. Miami is known for its vibrant lifestyle and beautiful coastlines, and yacht charters are seen as a way to enjoy that exclusivity. The prices reflected the comfort, privacy, and highquality service you get. For many visitors and residents celebrating special occasions, it's a memorable experience worth the cost. But it's not just for the wealthy. There are more affordable options like smaller boats or shorter trips which make the experience accessible to more people. You can rent a boat for just a few hours or even a couple of hours, often for less than $100. Prices also change depending on the season, with summer and holiday periods usually costing more. Booking in advance or on weekdays can sometimes save you money, too. In the end, Miami yacht charters are generally seen as a luxury activity, but they do come with a price tag that matches the high-end experience. Still, there are choices for different budgets, and the city's many marinas support a variety of water activities. Whether you want a quick ride or a full day of luxury, you can find options that fit your wallet and give you a chance to enjoy Miami's stunning waters. [Music]

- Why Do You Need A Crew On A Miami Yacht Charter? - Everything About Miami: Kind: captions Language: en Why do you need a crew on a Miami yacht charter? Imagine cruising through Miami's sparkling waters, feeling the warm breeze, and enjoying the stunning skyline. Now, picture doing all that without any help. Sounds stressful, right? That's why having a crew on your yacht charter makes all the difference. A crew is there to keep everyone safe and ensure the boat runs smoothly. Miami has strict rules from the United States Coast Guard, which requiring licensed captains and trained crew members to operate larger vessels legally and safely. The captain is in charge of navigation, making sure the yacht follows maritime laws, and keeping everyone safe on board. Without a captain, you cannot legally or safely operate a large yacht, which is common in Miami's water scene. The crew supports the captain by taking care of many tasks. They handle boat maintenance, docking, and anchoring, so you don't have to worry about any of that. They also assist with water activities like jet skiing, paddle boarding, or snorkeling, making sure everyone stays safe while having fun. Having a crew also boosts your comfort. Stewards or stewardises keep the interior spotless and cater to your needs. Whether it's preparing snacks or drinks, chefs can whip up delicious meals tailored to your tastes so you don't have to lift a finger. Deck hands help with outdoor activities and assist with setting up equipment for water sports, making your day on the water more enjoyable and hassle-free. Most Miami yacht charters can host up to 13 guests, not counting the crew. The crew enforces safety rules about alcohol and drugs, creating a secure environment for everyone. They also ensure the boat stays within weight and passenger limits, so the vessel remains stable and safe. In practical terms, the crew lets you relax and soak in Miami's beautiful coastline, lively waters, and iconic skyline. You won't need to worry about navigation, safety, or managing the vessel. Their experience guarantees your trip is not only luxurious, but also safe and compliant with all local and federal rules. That's why having a crew on your Miami yacht charter is essential for a memorable, worry-free water adventure. [Music]

get a boat capable of doing this trip the total amount of money that i had to put into this boat before we left for the trip was 11 956 dollars this number reflects material only since i was able to shaft packing for the rudders and the prop shafts and much much more so now for the question that i've heard again and again how much fuel did it really take to get this boat from maine to florida our was in chesapeake city maryland and it was three dollars and 10 cents per gallon some of the most expensive fuel we got was in atlantic city that was four dollars and 20 cents per gallon now fuel is rising and uh it's going to make this trip a little bit more expensive our average fuel cost down the entire east coast was 3.62 cents per gallon my boat is designated as what's called a fast trawler meaning the hole is a modified v-hull type boat it is also known as a semi-planing hole this is so that you can cruise at a faster speed than a full displacement trawler well you might be wondering why would you buy a slow trawler if you could buy a fast trawler well one of the main sacrifices is that you lose your full peel a full kill helps a lot with stability so when you don't have one you lose some stability also the keel provides some prop protection for this boat that doesn't seem to be much of an issue however we decided to take the cruise at full displacement speed which immensely helped with our fuel savings my average cruise speed was eight knots at 1300 rpm with an average fuel burn of four gallons per hour some of you may remember the day that we cruised up the delaware bay to the cnd canal we had to get some miles under our belt so that we wouldn't get stuck in the delaware bay that day we ran the boat at 16 knots at 2200 rpm just to give you an idea of the difference in fuel burn at that speed we burned an average of 32 gallons per hour that day that was an expensive day but our total fuel costs for the trip reflect that day as well one of the biggest costs for people that are traveling by boat is transient dockage and transient mortgage that is one thing that we did not spend a lot of money on we mostly anchored this resulted in insane cost savings when it came to transient dockage a lot of boaters like the security of being tied up to a dock at the end of their trip and they don't have to worry about dragging their anchor they're also supplied with shore power fresh water and other amenities of being at a marina in my opinion anchoring is what really ties together an amazing boating trip we anchored in extremely remote places like sandhol creek in north carolina you go outside and there is nothing around you it is great and the best part about it it's free if you wanted to factor transient dockage into your trip expenses you'd need to consider at least two to four dollars per foot per marina that you stop at the pricing depends mostly on the location of the marina my total transient dockage and mortgage costs were 371 dollars on the entire trip down the coast the only places that we paid for mortgage and dockage were new york city it was 120 for the night we wanted to go into the city and we weren't comfortable leaving the boat on anchor so we could have the peace of mind of leaving the boat safe while we explored the city in moorhead city north carolina we paid 20 dollars to stay on the sanitary restaurant dock we did this because it just made sense in saint augustine we rented a mooring for 26 because it was rather windy and we wanted to go to shore once we got to miami we spent 205 dollars on a mooring for a week another huge expense on any boat trip is maintenance costs i made sure my boat was in tip-top shape before we left for the trip which resulted in very few issues one of the issues that i had was one of my rebuilt raw water pumps fell apart unfortunately i check my engine bay at least once a day while we're underway and i noticed this pump was leaking before it completely failed so i decided to just buy a brand new pump 450 raw water pump most of the issues that the boat had on the trip were small issues that did not require any parts to fix rather just some labor and diagnostics a lot of repairs are what i'd call labor only repairs wiring issues little leaks things that just need a hose clamp and a new terminal saltwater boats have this inherent issue with corroding parts everything turns to crap in salt water anybody can fix their own boat it's just a matter of taking the time to go on the internet and learn about fixing your own boat if you do this you will save a ton of money i did not keep track of the hours that i put into my boat labor-wise however i would estimate at the very least i put 250 hours worth of labor into my boat i would say the average marina charges about 100 to 120 dollars per hour for their time yeah do the math on that one my only other maintenance cost on this entire trip was one oil change that i did in west palm beach that oil change cost me 178 dollars if i were to have done this trip on a sailboat i could cut my fuel costs at least in half if not even more one totally avoidable expense that i'll call personal pleasure which includes going
